message.content:
write a document describes how /optimize skill works 
including the script they use.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. Target VPU, toolchain, build flags, FPGA board, and hardware profile all come from config.toml — no hardcoded paths or CPU names. Runs a 7-step FSM — validate, select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
- hot-inner-kernel-extractor: Extract a libnn function's hot inner-most loop into a standalone, mcycle-wrapped RISC-V assembly harness so it can be measured on a cycle-accurate Verilator simulator (or any cycle counter) without running the whole function. Use this skill whenever the user wants to study pipeline behavior of a libnn kernel, profile inner-loop cycles, build a microbenchmark from an existing function, prepare a Verilator workload, isolate a hot loop body, or asks about "extracting", "isolating", "harnessing", "microbenchmark", "Verilator timeview", "pipeline timeview", "steady state", or "inner kernel" of any libnn / RVV function. The skill identifies the hottest translation block via QEMU's hotblocks plugin, walks objdump to find the innermost backward branch, computes live-in registers, and emits a compilable .S harness that runs the body N times under csrr mcycle.
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
1	---
2	name: optimize
3	description: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. Target VPU, toolchain, build flags, FPGA board, and hardware profile all come from config.toml — no hardcoded paths or CPU names. Runs a 7-step FSM — validate, select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
4	argument-hint: [validate|function_name|status|reset]
5	disable-model-invocation: false
6	allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Edit, Write, Agent
7	---
8	
9	# libnn RVV Optimization Skill
10	
11	Fully autonomous optimization loop. Target (VPU, toolchain, FPGA, CFLAGS) is configured in `.claude/skills/optimize/config.toml`. State persisted in the `state_file` path set there (default: `.claude/skills/optimize/state.json`).
12	
13	## Current State
14	
15	!`LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel); SKILL_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize"; source "${SKILL_DIR}/script/parse_config.sh" 2>/dev/null; cat "${CFG_STATE_FILE:-${SKILL_DIR}/state.json}" 2>/dev/null || echo '{"step":"idle","function":"","round":0,"history":[]}'`
16	
17	## Commands
18	
19	- `/optimize validate` — Run config.toml validation (Step 0) only
20	- `/optimize <function_name>` — Start optimizing a function (validates first, then loops until flat land)
21	- `/optimize status` — Show current state
22	- `/optimize reset` — Reset to idle
23	
24	Arguments received: `$ARGUMENTS`
25	
26	## Finite State Machine
27	
28	```
29	idle → validate → select → analyze ──→ implement → verify ──→ measure ──→ learn ─┐
30	          |         ↑  |         ↑          |  |                                  |
31	          |         |  |         └──────────┘  |                                  |
32	          |         |  |      (test fail → fix)|                                  |
33	          |         |  |                       └→ analyze                         |
34	          |         |  |                (3 retries exhausted)                     |
35	          |         |  └→ idle (no opportunity found)                             |
36	          |         |                                                             |
37	          |         └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
38	          |           (loop with new insights + assembly analysis)
39	          └→ idle (config validation failed)
40	
41	Exit conditions:
42	  - validate fails → idle (fix config.toml, retry)
43	  - analyze finds no optimization opportunity → idle
44	  - max [limits].max_rounds per function → idle (5 by default; see config.toml)
45	```
46	
47	The loop is the core mechanic: after `learn`, go back to `analyze` with fresh insights from FPGA measurements, updated assembly, and recomputed metrics. The verify/measure loop provides real feedback.
48	
49	---
50	
51	## Build Scripts Reference
52	
53	All scripts live under the skill itself at `.claude/skills/optimize/script/`. They each source `parse_config.sh` to pull toolchain paths, CFLAGS, QEMU/FPGA targets, and work dir from `config.toml` — no hardcoded values. Always invoke with `/usr/bin/bash`.
54	
55	**Shell-variable convention for every bash block in this skill:**
56	```bash
57	LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
58	SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
59	```
60	When you need config values (work dir, toolchain, etc.) inside a bash block, `source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh"` and use the exported `CFG_*` variables (e.g. `${CFG_WORK_DIR}`, `${CFG_CC}`, `${CFG_CPU}`).
61	
62	| Script | Purpose | Time |
63	|--------|---------|------|
64	| `validate_config.py` | 7-step fail-fast config validation (toolchain, QEMU, FPGA .inc parse, FPGA TCP, GDB run). Used by `validate` state. | ~10s |
65	| `build_libnn.sh` | Full rebuild of libnn.a (~400 files) using `${CFG_CPU}`. Use ONLY for initial build in `select` step. | ~30s |
66	| `rebuild_single.sh <src.c>` | Recompile single .c with `${CFG_CFLAGS_BASE}`, update libnn.a + objdump. Use for ALL rebuilds after `implement`. | ~0.6s |
67	| `rebuild_single.sh -E <src.c>` | Preprocess only (same CFLAGS). Output: `${CFG_WORK_DIR}/<basename>.i`. Use in `analyze` to see which `#ifdef` branches survive. | ~0.3s |
68	| `run_qemu_test.sh <name>` | Build test binary + run on QEMU with fd-9 strace capture + parse pass/fail. Uses `${CFG_QEMU_*}`. Use in `verify`. | ~2s |
69	| `run_fpga_test.sh <name>` | FPGA cycle count measurement via GDB. Uses `${CFG_FPGA_*}`. Use in `select` baseline + `measure`. | ~30s |
70	| `emit_trace.sh <sub>`    | Structured trace capture (Phase 0 meta-harness). Subcommands: `init`, `step`, `timing`, `fail`. Writes artifacts under `trace_corpus/<fn>/round_<N>/`. Auto-reads fn/round from state.json. | <1s |
71	
72	---
73	
74	## Trace Emission (Phase 0 meta-harness)
75	
76	After each reasoning step produces output, the main agent writes a structured
77	trace artifact to `trace_corpus/<fn>/round_<N>/` via `${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh`.
78	These artifacts are the input to the outer meta-loop's harness-critic (future phase).
79	Trace emission does NOT change FSM behavior.
80	
81	**Per-step artifacts:**
82	
83	| Step | Artifact | Contents |
84	|------|----------|----------|
85	| analyze   | `analyze_agent.md`    | Subagent hypothesis, ranked ideas, AI/reuse metrics |
86	| implement | `implement_agent.md`  | Subagent's description of the exact code change |
87	| verify    | `verify_result.json`  | `{"result": "PASS\|FAIL\|COMPILE_ERROR\|TIMEOUT\|UNKNOWN", "sub_tests": N, "stderr": "..."}` |
88	| measure   | `measure_result.json` | `{"cycles": N, "instrs": M, "ipc": X, "speedup": Y}` |
89	| learn     | `learn_agent.md`      | Lessons written to wiki this round (summary) |
90	| (any)     | `fail_reason.md`      | Tagged failure class: `compile_error`, `test_fail`, `regression`, `timeout`, `unknown` |
91	
92	Timing (wall-clock per step) is appended to `step_timings.json` automatically by
93	the state-transition block — no explicit call needed.
94	
95	**Emission pattern** (auto-mode reads fn/round from state.json; no flags needed):
96	
97	```bash
98	LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
99	SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
100	# Write payload to a temp file, then emit:
101	cat > /tmp/trace_payload <<'EOF'
102	<content>
103	EOF
104	/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh step <step_name> /tmp/trace_payload
105	# Or on failure:
106	/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh fail <tag> /tmp/error_stderr
107	```
108	
109	No trace is emitted from `validate` or `select` — trace lifecycle begins at
110	`analyze` (which runs in a round context).
111	
112	---
113	
114	## State: `idle`
115	
116	No optimization in progress.
117	
118	Routing:
119	- `/optimize validate` → `validate` (run 7-step check, then return to `idle`)
120	- `/optimize <function_name>` → `validate` first (cache-aware); on PASS, continue to `select`; on FAIL, stay `idle`
121	- `/optimize status` → report current state, stay `idle`
122	- `/optimize reset` → clear state, stay `idle`
123	
124	---
125	
126	## State: `validate`
127	
128	Check that `config.toml` points at a working toolchain, reachable QEMU, parseable FPGA .inc, and live FPGA board BEFORE any optimization work starts. Failing fast here saves a lot of pain later.
129	
130	1. Cache check (skip re-validation if nothing has changed):
131	   ```bash
132	   LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
133	   SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
134	   source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh"
135	   # Skip if last validated within [limits].validate_cache_hours AND
136	   # config.toml + .inc mtimes are not newer than state.validated_at
137	   ```
138	   If cached validation is still fresh, skip to step 4.
139	
140	2. Run the 7-step validator:
141	   ```bash
142	   python3 "${SCRIPT_DIR}/validate_config.py"
143	   ```
144	   The script prints `[OK]` / `[FAIL]` per step and exits non-zero on the first failure. The 7 steps are:
145	   1. Load `config.toml` — all required sections/keys present
146	   2. Toolchain — `${CFG_TOOLCHAIN_BIN}` exists, `${CFG_CC}` accepts Andes flags
147	   3. Compile hello world — produces valid RISC-V ELF
148	   4. QEMU — `${CFG_QEMU_BIN}` runs hello.adx (strace-captured semihosting)
149	   5. FPGA .inc parse — `${CFG_FPGA_CONFIG}` parses (VLEN, caches, ISA features)
150	   6. FPGA TCP — connect to `${CFG_FPGA_HOST}:${CFG_FPGA_PORT}` (5s timeout)
151	   7. FPGA GDB run — compile hello_fpga and run it on the board
152	
153	3. On `[FAIL]`:
154	   - Report which step failed and what needs fixing in `config.toml` (or auxiliary files like the .inc path).
155	   - Steps 6 / 7 may fail if the FPGA board is unreachable from the current network. When that happens and the user only needs QEMU-only work, they can skip by setting `[limits].validate_cache_hours` high after a prior success, or by running `/optimize validate` from a network with FPGA access.
156	   - Transition → `idle`. Do NOT proceed to `select`.
157	
158	4. On `[OK]` (or cache hit): update state:
159	   ```json
160	   {"validated_at": "<ISO timestamp>", "config_toml_mtime": <stat mtime>}
161	   ```
162	   If `/optimize validate` was called directly → transition → `idle` with a success summary.
163	   If `/optimize <function>` triggered validation → transition → `select`.
164	
165	**Transition:** → `select` (when invoked via function-name path) or → `idle`.
166	
167	---
168	
169	## State: `select`
170	
171	Validate the function and gather baseline context.
172	
173	1. Find the source file under `Source/` — grep for the function name
174	2. Confirm it has `#ifdef ENA_VEC_ISA` (existing RVV to improve)
175	3. Find corresponding test in `Examples/unit_func/t_<name>.c`
176	4. Query the wiki for prior lessons and applicable techniques:
177	   - Read `docs/wiki/index.md` to identify relevant pages
178	   - Read the operator page in `docs/wiki/operators/` if it exists (e.g., `conv-hwc-s8-sym-bias-fast.md`)
179	   - Read matching `docs/wiki/general/` pages for applicable techniques and known anti-patterns
180	5. Full build (first time only):
181	   ```bash
182	   /usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/build_libnn.sh
183	   ```
184	6. Run baseline FPGA measurement (**MUST use this exact script — do NOT compile or run FPGA tests manually**):
185	   ```bash
186	   /usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/run_fpga_test.sh <test_name>
187	   ```
188	   Parse both counts from the output lines (same format as `measure` state):
189	   - `"The cycle count is <N>"` → `cycles_before`
190	   - `"The inst count is <N>"` → `instrs_before`
191	   (The `RESULT: PASS (cycles=N)` summary line also carries cycles but not instrs, so use the raw lines for both.)
192	7. **Identify the active code path.** Primary: preprocess the source with the same build flags and read the surviving `#ifdef` branches:
193	   ```bash
194	   /usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/rebuild_single.sh -E <source_file>
195	   # Output: ${CFG_WORK_DIR}/<basename>.i
196	   ```
197	   Secondary (for dispatch functions that call sub-kernels): confirm the linked sub-function names from the perf objdump:
198	   ```bash
199	   grep -A500 '<function_name>:' ${CFG_WORK_DIR}/t_<test>_perf.objdump | \
200	     grep -E "(jal|jalr).*<[^+>]+>" | head -20
201	   ```
202	   Save `active_path_file` (path to `.i`) and `called_functions` in state.
203	8. Save state: function, source_file, test_name, cycles_before, instrs_before, active_path_file, called_functions.
204	9. **Capture round-0 baseline checkpoint.** Snapshot the current working tree (with any prior-session uncommitted edits) so any future round can restore to this clean baseline:
205	   ```bash
206	   LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
207	   FN=<function_name>
208	   REF="refs/optimize-ckpt/${FN}/round-0-baseline"
209	   SHA=$(cd "${LIB_ROOT}" && git stash create "optimize ${FN} round-0 baseline")
210	   if [ -n "${SHA}" ]; then
211	     git -C "${LIB_ROOT}" update-ref "${REF}" "${SHA}"
212	   else
213	     git -C "${LIB_ROOT}" update-ref "${REF}" HEAD
214	   fi
215	   # Persist in state for diagnostics
216	   python3 - "${CFG_STATE_FILE}" "${REF}" <<'PYEOF'
217	   import json,sys
218	   f, ref = sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2]
219	   s = json.load(open(f))
220	   s['baseline_ref'] = ref
221	   json.dump(s, open(f,'w'), indent=2)
222	   PYEOF
223	   ```
224	   These refs live under `refs/optimize-ckpt/<fn>/...` — invisible to `git branch` / `git tag`, won't pollute history, but are inspectable via `git for-each-ref refs/optimize-ckpt/`.
225	
226	**Transition:** → `analyze`
227	**Fail:** Function not found or no RVV → stay `select`, report error.
228	
229	---
230	
231	## State: `analyze`
232	
233	Study the current RVV code and assembly to form the next optimization hypothesis.
234	
235	**CRITICAL — Identify the active code path from preprocessed source, not from raw `#ifdef` guards.**
236	
237	Many `#ifdef` guards depend on macros defined in internal headers (e.g., `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO` are unconditionally defined in `internal/internal_nn_math.h`), NOT just build.sh flags. **Never guess the active path from raw source alone.** Instead:
238	
239	1. **Primary method — preprocessed source.** Run the file through the preprocessor with the exact build flags:
240	   ```bash
241	   /usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/rebuild_single.sh -E <source_file.c>
242	   # Output: ${CFG_WORK_DIR}/<basename>.i
243	   ```
244	   The `.i` file contains only the branches that survive preprocessing — it directly answers "which `#ifdef` block is active" without inference. Grep for the function in the `.i` file and read that body. This works even when the active block is fully inline (no sub-function calls), which objdump cannot disambiguate.
245	
246	2. **Secondary method — objdump cross-check.** For functions that dispatch to sub-kernels, confirm by checking which sub-functions the linked binary actually calls:
247	   ```bash
248	   grep -E "(jal|jalr).*<[^+>]+>" ${CFG_WORK_DIR}/t_<test>_perf.objdump | grep -v "gdb\|nds\|start\|__" | head -20
249	   ```
250	   Cross-reference call targets (e.g., `nn_mat_mul_kernel_tiling_q7` vs `nn_mat_mul_kernel_q7_bias_2sft`) with the preprocessed source. This catches cases where LTO/dead-code elimination pruned a branch that `-E` still shows.
251	
252	3. When mapping the `.i` body back to raw source for editing, target only the `#ifdef` branch whose body matches the `.i`. Modifying any other branch has zero effect.
253	
254	**Active-defines hint.** For a quick list of which macros `config.toml` declares defined vs undefined, source `parse_config.sh` and inspect `${CFG_DEFINES_DEFINED}` and `${CFG_DEFINES_UNDEFINED}` (from `[target.active_defines]`). These are a hint only — the `.i` file is always authoritative, because internal headers can redefine things without any config.toml knowledge.
255	
256	### Analysis
257	
258	**On first round (round=1):**
259	1. Identify the active code path from the `.i` file saved in `active_path_file` by `select`. Read the function body there to understand what will actually execute.
260	2. Find the function's assembly in `libnn.objdump` (regenerated by `rebuild_single.sh` after every compile; located at `${CFG_LIBNN_ROOT}/libnn.objdump`):
261	   ```bash
262	   grep -A 200 '<function_name>:' ${CFG_LIBNN_ROOT}/libnn.objdump | head -300
263	   ```
264	3. If the function calls sub-functions (GEMM kernels, etc.), run `${SCRIPT_DIR}/rebuild_single.sh -E` on each sub-function's source file too, and read its assembly from `libnn.objdump`. Sub-functions have their own `#ifdef` branches.
265	4. Query the wiki: read `docs/wiki/index.md`, relevant `general/` pages for similar patterns, operator page if it exists
266	5. Compute key metrics from the assembly and source:
267	   - **Arithmetic Intensity** = FLOPs per byte transferred from memory. Count MAC-equivalent ops (e.g., each `vqmacc` contributes `2 * vl` FLOPs for LMUL-dependent `vl`); count bytes loaded/stored per loop iteration (load instructions x element width x vl). AI = FLOPs / bytes.
268	   - **Reuse Rate** = FLOPs per byte loaded (exclude stores). Measures how many times each loaded byte contributes to computation before being evicted. High reuse = cache-friendly; low reuse = memory-bound.
269	   - Save both as `arith_intensity` and `reuse_rate` in state.
270	6. Identify bottleneck using the metrics: low AI -> memory-bound (improve tiling/blocking/reuse); high AI but low IPC -> compute-bound (LMUL, register pressure, loop structure)
271	7. Form hypothesis: what to change and expected impact
272	
273	**On subsequent rounds (round>1):**
274	1. Re-run `${SCRIPT_DIR}/rebuild_single.sh -E <source_file>` to refresh the `.i` (the previous round's `implement` modified the source, and if any macros were touched the `#ifdef` resolution may have shifted). Re-read the active block.
275	2. Re-check `${CFG_LIBNN_ROOT}/libnn.objdump` for the updated assembly of the function.
276	3. Recompute arithmetic intensity and reuse rate from the updated assembly. Compare to previous round — did the change improve reuse or reduce memory traffic?
277	4. Review FPGA cycle/instruction delta from last round's `measure` step to identify new bottleneck
278	5. Query the wiki: read `docs/wiki/index.md` and relevant `general/` pages for the specific bottleneck pattern
279	6. Form the next hypothesis based on what the metrics reveal
280	
281	**Proposer output:** 1–3 ranked ideas, each with what to change, which lines, expected impact rationale, and risk assessment. Include current arithmetic intensity and reuse rate in the summary. Pick the top idea as the hypothesis.
282	
283	### Output and Transitions
284	
285	**Output:** Print the analysis summary (including arithmetic intensity, reuse rate, and hypothesis). Do NOT wait for approval — proceed immediately.
286	
287	**Trace emit:** Write the subagent's full analysis (hypothesis, AI, reuse rate, ranked ideas, risk) to a tmp file, then:
288	```bash
289	/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh step analyze /tmp/analyze_trace.md
290	```
291	
292	**Decision gate:** If no concrete optimization opportunity can be identified (assembly looks clean, metrics are already optimal, LMUL/tiling are already well-tuned):
293	- Transition to idle with final summary.
294	- Do NOT force an optimization that has no clear rationale.
295	
296	**Transition:** → `implement` (if opportunity found) or → `idle` (if no opportunity)
297	
298	---
299	
300	## State: `implement`
301	
302	Apply the optimization.
303	
304	**Step 0 — capture a checkpoint of the working tree BEFORE any edit.** This is the rollback point if the round regresses. Uses `git stash create` (snapshot blob; does NOT modify the working tree, index, or branch state) plus `git update-ref` (anchors the blob so git GC won't collect it). One ref per round per function.
305	
306	```bash
307	LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
308	SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
309	source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh"
310	FN=$(python3 -c "import json;print(json.load(open('${CFG_STATE_FILE}'))['function'])")
311	ROUND=$(python3 -c "import json;print(json.load(open('${CFG_STATE_FILE}'))['round'])")
312	REF="refs/optimize-ckpt/${FN}/round-${ROUND}-pre"
313	SHA=$(cd "${LIB_ROOT}" && git stash create "optimize ${FN} round-${ROUND} pre-implement")
314	if [ -n "${SHA}" ]; then
315	  git -C "${LIB_ROOT}" update-ref "${REF}" "${SHA}"
316	else
317	  # No tracked-file changes vs HEAD: anchor HEAD itself so revert works
318	  git -C "${LIB_ROOT}" update-ref "${REF}" HEAD
319	fi
320	# Persist the ref name in state for the measure step's auto-revert
321	python3 - "${CFG_STATE_FILE}" "${REF}" <<'PYEOF'
322	import json,sys
323	f, ref = sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2]
324	s = json.load(open(f))
325	s['checkpoint_ref'] = ref
326	json.dump(s, open(f,'w'), indent=2)
327	PYEOF
328	```
329	
330	Then perform the edit:
331	
332	1. Modify ONLY the active code path identified during `analyze`. The active path was determined from the preprocessed `.i` file (with objdump cross-check for dispatch functions). **Do NOT guess from `#ifdef` guards in the raw source** — many macros (e.g., `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO`) are defined in internal headers, not build.sh. Map from the `.i` body back to the corresponding raw-source line numbers when editing.
333	2. If the function calls sub-functions (e.g., a GEMM kernel), the optimization may need to be applied in the sub-function's source file, not the conv function's source.
334	3. Do NOT touch the plain C fallback or dead `#ifdef` blocks
335	4. Save a description of what was changed in state (`changes` field), AND list the modified files in state under `changed_files: [...]` so `measure` knows what to revert on regression.
336	5. If this is round>1 and the previous round regressed, the prior round's `measure` step has ALREADY auto-reverted via the checkpoint; the working tree is back to the pre-regression state. Just proceed with the new approach.
337	
338	**CRITICAL — Intrinsic API selection:**
339	The active `ENA_VEC_ISA` code path uses **`NDS_VEC_*` macros** (e.g., `NDS_VEC_VSETVLI`, `NDS_VEC_VLE8_V`, `NDS_VEC_VQMACC_VX`, `NDS_VEC_VNCLIP_WX`). These are defined in `internal/internal_vec_isa_v*.h` (version chosen by `NDS_VEC_RVV_VERSION` in `[target.cflags_base]`).
340	
341	**NEVER use `__riscv_*` C intrinsics** (e.g., `__riscv_vle8_v_i8m2`, `__riscv_vqmacc_vx_i32m8`) in `ENA_VEC_ISA` blocks. Those require `ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC`, which is listed under `[target.active_defines].undefined` in `config.toml` for this target. Using them causes compilation failures.
342	
343	The canonical reference for legal macros is `${CFG_INTRINSIC_API}` (path from `[target].intrinsic_api`). Read that file before writing new RVV code. In addition:
344	- Study an existing working function in the same `#ifdef` block to see live usage patterns
345	- Use explicit register names (`NDS_VEC_V0`, `NDS_VEC_V8`, `NDS_VEC_V16`, etc.)
346	- Match the LMUL register grouping: M8 uses v0/v8/v16/v24, M4 uses v0/v4/v8/.., M2 uses v0/v2/v4/..
347	- For loads: `NDS_VEC_VLE8_V` (e8), `NDS_VEC_VLE16_V` (e16), `NDS_VEC_VLE32_V` (e32)
348	- For stores: `NDS_VEC_VSE8_V` (e8), `NDS_VEC_VSE16_V` (e16), `NDS_VEC_VSE32_V` (e32)
349	
350	**Trace emit:** After the implement subagent returns, write its description of the exact change (which file, which lines, what replaced what, reasoning) to a tmp file, then:
351	```bash
352	/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh step implement /tmp/implement_trace.md
353	```
354	
355	**Transition:** → `verify`
356	
357	---
358	
359	## State: `verify`
360	
361	Incremental rebuild and QEMU correctness test — all via scripts.
362	
363	1. Rebuild ONLY the modified libnn source (fast incremental):
364	   ```bash
365	   /usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/rebuild_single.sh <source_file>
366	   ```
367	
368	2. Build the test binary AND run it on QEMU with fd-9 semihosting capture in one script call:
369	   ```bash
370	   /usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/run_qemu_test.sh <test_name>
371	   ```
372	   The script reads `${CFG_QEMU_*}` + `${CFG_CFLAGS_BASE}` + `${CFG_CFLAGS_VERIFY}` from `config.toml`, compiles `Examples/unit_func/t_<test_name>.c` against `libnn.a`, runs under `strace -e trace=write` to capture fd-9 semihosting, and emits one of:
373	   - `RESULT: PASS (N sub-tests passed)` — correctness verified
374	   - `RESULT: FAIL (N failures detected)` — compiled but wrong results
375	   - `RESULT: COMPILE_ERROR` — gcc failed; full error is on stderr
376	   - `RESULT: TIMEOUT (Ns)` — QEMU hang
377	   - `RESULT: UNKNOWN (...)` — no pass/fail marker in output
378	
379	3. Do NOT run full regression in the optimization loop — only the target function's test.
380	
381	**Trace emit:** After `run_qemu_test.sh` returns, capture the result to a JSON payload (one-shot, regardless of pass/fail):
382	```bash
383	cat > /tmp/verify_trace.json <<EOF
384	{"result": "PASS|FAIL|COMPILE_ERROR|TIMEOUT|UNKNOWN", "sub_tests": <N>, "stderr": "<first few lines if any>"}
385	EOF
386	/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh step verify /tmp/verify_trace.json
387	```
388	On non-PASS also emit a failure tag (writes `fail_reason.md`). Tag mapping:
389	- `COMPILE_ERROR` → `compile_error` (pass gcc stderr as message file)
390	- `FAIL` → `test_fail`
391	- `TIMEOUT` → `timeout`
392	- `UNKNOWN` → `unknown`
393	```bash
394	/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh fail <tag> /tmp/qemu_stderr.txt
395	```
396	
397	**Pass:** → `measure`
398	**`COMPILE_ERROR`:** Launch ONE `error-fix` subagent with the full stderr + source file path. It reads the error and edits the source. Re-run step 1+2. Max 3 compile-fix retries before going back to `analyze`.
399	**`FAIL` (wrong results):** → `implement` (diagnose + fix). Max 3 test-fix retries before going back to `analyze`.
400	**`TIMEOUT` / `UNKNOWN`:** Treat as FAIL, same retry policy.
401	
402	---
403	
404	## State: `measure`
405	
406	Run on the FPGA board via GDB for ground-truth cycle counts.
407	
408	```bash
409	/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/run_fpga_test.sh <test_name>
410	```
411	
412	This script reads `[target].cpu`, `[target.cflags_base]`, `[target.cflags_perf]`, `[fpga].*` from `config.toml` and:
413	1. Compiles the test with `${CFG_CFLAGS_BASE} ${CFG_CFLAGS_PERF}` (includes `-DPF_COUNTER -DENA_TEST_PERF -DENA_RUN_TWICE` on the perf list)
414	2. Uploads binary to FPGA via the GDB expect script at `${CFG_FPGA_SIM_WRAPPER}`
415	3. Runs on the board at `${CFG_FPGA_HOST}:${CFG_FPGA_PORT}`
416	4. Generates `.adx` and `.objdump` in `${CFG_WORK_DIR}/`
417	
418	Parse the output for:
419	- `"The cycle count is <N>"` — FPGA cycle count
420	- `"The inst count is <N>"` — FPGA instruction count
421	- `"accuracy checking pass/fail"` — correctness check
422	
423	Save `cycles_after` and `instrs_after` in state. Compute speedup: `cycles_before / cycles_after`. Compute IPC: `instrs_after / cycles_after`.
424	
425	**Trace emit:** After parsing, write a JSON payload and emit:
426	```bash
427	cat > /tmp/measure_trace.json <<EOF
428	{"cycles": <cycles_after>, "instrs": <instrs_after>, "ipc": <ipc>, "speedup": <speedup>, "cycles_before": <cycles_before>}
429	EOF
430	/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh step measure /tmp/measure_trace.json
431	```
432	If speedup < 1.0 (regression), also emit a failure tag AND **auto-restore the working tree from the pre-implement checkpoint captured in `implement` step 0**:
433	```bash
434	echo "cycles regressed: ${cycles_before} -> ${cycles_after} (speedup ${speedup})" > /tmp/regression.txt
435	/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh fail regression /tmp/regression.txt
436	
437	# AUTO-REVERT from checkpoint
438	LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
439	source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh"
440	REF=$(python3 -c "import json;print(json.load(open('${CFG_STATE_FILE}'))['checkpoint_ref'])")
441	FILES=$(python3 -c "import json;print(' '.join(json.load(open('${CFG_STATE_FILE}')).get('changed_files',[])))")
442	if [ -n "${REF}" ] && [ -n "${FILES}" ]; then
443	  git -C "${LIB_ROOT}" checkout "${REF}" -- ${FILES}
444	  /usr/bin/bash "${SCRIPT_DIR}/rebuild_single.sh" ${FILES}  # rebuild from restored source
445	  echo "Auto-reverted ${FILES} from ${REF}; libnn.a is back to pre-round-${ROUND} state."
446	else
447	  echo "WARN: no checkpoint_ref or changed_files in state — manual revert needed."
448	fi
449	```
450	
451	**Transition:** → `learn`
452	
453	---
454	
455	## State: `learn`
456	
457	Record the lesson, then loop back.
458	
459	1. Update the wiki following the wiki-writer skill:
460	   - Read `.claude/skills/wiki-writer/SKILL.md`
461	   - Input context from state: function, source_file, round, hypothesis, changes, cycles_before, cycles_after, speedup, arith_intensity, reuse_rate, outcome
462	   - Execute all 4 operations: operator page, general page (if applicable), index, log
463	   - All content must follow the naive expression style defined in wiki-writer
464	
465	2. **Trace emit** (before the round counter bumps, so attribution stays on this round):
466	   ```bash
467	   cat > /tmp/learn_trace.md <<EOF
468	   <1-3 sentence summary of lessons recorded this round; cite which wiki pages updated>
469	   EOF
470	   /usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh step learn /tmp/learn_trace.md
471	   ```
472	
473	3. **Commit the round atomically.** A round whose `verify` PASSED and whose `measure` was not a regression (`speedup >= 1.0`) is "good enough to keep" -- commit it so the git history mirrors `round_history`, and the next round starts from a clean working tree. The commit covers ONLY the files listed in `state.changed_files` (the perf-relevant edits) -- never `git add -A` (that would drag in skill state, work-dir artifacts, wiki edits, etc.; wiki commits happen separately if at all). Regressed rounds were already reverted in `measure`, so there is nothing to commit -- skip this step.
474	   ```bash
475	   LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
476	   SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
477	   source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh"
478	   # Source state into shell vars (python writes a sourceable file -- safe across
479	   # multi-word approach strings, paths with spaces, etc.).
480	   python3 - "${CFG_STATE_FILE}" > /tmp/optimize_commit_env <<'PYEOF'
481	import json,sys,shlex
482	s=json.load(open(sys.argv[1]))
483	last=s['round_history'][-1] if s.get('round_history') else {}
484	def emit(k,v): print(f"{k}={shlex.quote(str(v))}")
485	emit('FN',       s.get('function',''))
486	emit('ROUND',    s.get('round',''))
487	emit('APPROACH', last.get('approach',''))
488	emit('CYC_BEFORE', s.get('cycles_before',''))
489	emit('CYC_AFTER',  s.get('cycles_after',''))
490	emit('SPEEDUP',  s.get('speedup',''))
491	emit('OUTCOME',  last.get('outcome',''))
492	emit('FILES',    ' '.join(s.get('changed_files',[])))
493	PYEOF
494	   . /tmp/optimize_commit_env
495	   if [ "${OUTCOME}" = "improved" ] || [ "${OUTCOME}" = "neutral" ]; then
496	     if [ -n "${FILES}" ]; then
497	       git -C "${LIB_ROOT}" add -- ${FILES}
498	       git -C "${LIB_ROOT}" commit -m "$(cat <<COMMIT
499	[opt/rvv] ${FN}: round ${ROUND} -- ${APPROACH}
500	
501	Cycles ${CYC_BEFORE} -> ${CYC_AFTER} (${SPEEDUP}x). Outcome: ${OUTCOME}.
502	Test: t_${FN} 3/3 sub-tests pass on FPGA-measured binary.
503	
504	Co-Authored-By: optimize-skill <noreply@anthropic.com>
505	COMMIT
506	)" || echo "WARN: commit failed (likely no staged changes -- e.g. asm-only reorder elided by compiler, or files already committed)"
507	     else
508	       echo "WARN: no changed_files in state -- skipping commit"
509	     fi
510	   else
511	     echo "Skipping commit: outcome=${OUTCOME} (regression was reverted in measure step)"
512	   fi
513	   ```
514	
515	4. Increment the `round` counter in state.
516	
517	5. **Check exit condition:**
518	   - If `round >= ${CFG_MAX_ROUNDS}` (from `[limits].max_rounds` in config.toml, default 5), STOP → `idle`. Print final summary of all rounds.
519	   - Otherwise, transition → `analyze` for the next optimization round on the same function.
520	
521	6. When looping back to `analyze`, key inputs for the next round are: FPGA cycle/instruction counts from `measure`, updated arithmetic intensity and reuse rate from this round, updated assembly, and wiki pages. The `analyze` step will decide whether there's still an opportunity. If not, it exits to `idle`.
522	
523	**Why commit per-round (rationale for step 3):** the working tree should never carry more than one round's worth of unverified change. If round N+1 introduces a subtle regression that the FPGA test misses (e.g., shape-dependent edge case found later by full regression), `git log --oneline` reads as a punch-list of speedups and `git revert <round-N+1-sha>` restores the last good state in one shot. The pre-implement checkpoint ref at `refs/optimize-ckpt/<fn>/round-N-pre` is for the in-loop auto-revert (regression caught in `measure`); the per-round commit is for after-the-fact bisection.
524	
525	**Exit → `idle`:** Print a summary table of all rounds (round#, approach, cycles, instrs, IPC, AI, reuse_rate, speedup, insight). Instruction count diagnoses whether gains came from fewer instructions (algorithmic) or better IPC (cache/scheduling).
526	**Continue → `analyze`:** Print "Round N+1: continuing. AI=X, reuse_rate=Y, key finding: <insight>"
527	
528	---
529	
530	## State File Format
531	
532	State lives at `${CFG_STATE_FILE}` (from `[repo].state_file` in config.toml — default `.claude/skills/optimize/state.json`).
533	
534	```json
535	{
536	  "step": "idle|validate|select|analyze|implement|verify|measure|learn",
537	  "function": "function_name",
538	  "source_file": "Source/.../file.c",
539	  "test_name": "test_name_for_qemu",
540	  "round": 0,
541	  "hypothesis": "what we plan to change and why",
542	  "changes": "description of what was implemented",
543	  "cycles_before": "",
544	  "cycles_after": "",
545	  "instrs_before": "",
546	  "instrs_after": "",
547	  "speedup": "",
548	  "arith_intensity": "",
549	  "reuse_rate": "",
550	  "active_path_file": "",
551	  "called_functions": [],
552	  "validated_at": "",
553	  "config_toml_mtime": 0,
554	  "trace_dir": "trace_corpus/<fn>/round_<N>/ (derivable from function+round; populated lazily)",
555	  "current_trace_round": "mirrors round; bookkeeping for the meta-harness outer loop",
556	  "round_history": [
557	    {
558	      "round": 1,
559	      "approach": "...",
560	      "cycles": "...",
561	      "instrs": "...",
562	      "ipc": "...",
563	      "arith_intensity": "...",
564	      "reuse_rate": "...",
565	      "speedup": "...",
566	      "outcome": "improved|regressed|neutral",
567	      "insight": "..."
568	    }
569	  ],
570	  "history": ["timestamps of state transitions"]
571	}
572	```
573	
574	Update state file on every transition (uses `CFG_STATE_FILE` from parse_config.sh — no hardcoded path). The block also auto-emits `step_timings.json` entries via `emit_trace.sh timing` for the step that just finished, so individual states do not need explicit timing calls:
575	```bash
576	LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
577	SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
578	source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh"
579	python3 - "${CFG_STATE_FILE}" "<new_step>" "${SCRIPT_DIR}" <<'PYEOF'
580	import json, os, sys, subprocess
581	from datetime import datetime
582	f, new_step = sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2]
583	script_dir = sys.argv[3] if len(sys.argv) > 3 else ""
584	os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(f), exist_ok=True)
585	state = json.load(open(f)) if os.path.exists(f) else {"step": "idle", "history": []}
586	prev_step = state.get("step", "idle")
587	now = datetime.now()
588	prev_start = datetime.fromisoformat(state.get("prev_step_started", now.isoformat()))
589	elapsed = (now - prev_start).total_seconds()
590	state["last_step_duration"] = f"{prev_step}: {elapsed:.1f}s"
591	# Auto-emit trace timing for traced FSM steps. `learn` bumps the round before
592	# this block runs, so attribute learn's timing to round-1.
593	traced = ("analyze", "implement", "verify", "measure", "learn")
594	fn_for_trace = state.get("function", "")
595	rnd_for_trace = state.get("round", 0)
596	if prev_step == "learn" and isinstance(rnd_for_trace, int) and rnd_for_trace > 0:
597	    rnd_for_trace -= 1
598	if script_dir and fn_for_trace and prev_step in traced:
599	    emit = os.path.join(script_dir, "emit_trace.sh")
600	    if os.path.isfile(emit):
601	        subprocess.run(
602	            [emit, "timing", "--fn", str(fn_for_trace),
603	             "--round", str(rnd_for_trace), prev_step, f"{elapsed:.3f}"],
604	            check=False,
605	        )
606	state["step"] = new_step
607	state["step_started"] = now.isoformat()
608	state["history"] = state.get("history", []) + [f"{now.isoformat()} {new_step}"]
609	state["prev_step_started"] = state["step_started"]
610	json.dump(state, open(f, "w"), indent=2)
611	print(json.dumps(state, indent=2))
612	PYEOF
613	```
614	
615	---
616	
617	## Subagent Dispatch
618	
619	The main agent drives the FSM and reports progress to the user between every step. Only steps that require reasoning use subagents. Mechanical steps run commands directly.
620	
621	| Step | Execution | Why |
622	|------|-----------|-----|
623	| **validate** | Main agent runs `python3 ${SCRIPT_DIR}/validate_config.py` | Mechanical check; no reasoning |
624	| **select** | Main agent runs commands directly | Just shell commands (grep, build, FPGA baseline, `-E` preprocess) |
625	| **analyze** | Subagent (proposer) | Requires reading code + assembly + metrics, forming hypothesis |
626	| **implement** | Subagent | Requires code editing with domain knowledge |
627	| **verify** | Main agent runs `rebuild_single.sh` + `run_qemu_test.sh`. On `COMPILE_ERROR`: single `error-fix` subagent reads error and edits source | Build + QEMU are fully scripted; only error diagnosis needs reasoning |
628	| **measure** | Main agent runs commands directly | Just `run_fpga_test.sh` |
629	| **learn** | Main agent runs commands directly | Wiki page updates + state update |
630	
631	### Subagent constraints
632	
633	When dispatching to a subagent, always include these constraints in the prompt:
634	
635	**For `analyze` subagent (proposer):**
636	- Dispatch: `Agent(model="opus", ...)`. Prepend the prompt with `[effort: xhigh]` so the subagent reasons at extended-thinking depth (this is the reasoning-heaviest step).
637	- Tools allowed: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash (ONLY for `grep` and `${SCRIPT_DIR}/rebuild_single.sh -E`)
638	- DO NOT edit any files
639	- DO NOT run compile/archive builds, test, QEMU, or FPGA commands. `rebuild_single.sh -E` (preprocess-only) IS allowed.
640	- **MUST identify the active code path from preprocessed source**, not raw source. Run `${SCRIPT_DIR}/rebuild_single.sh -E <src.c>` and read `${CFG_WORK_DIR}/<basename>.i` — only surviving `#ifdef` branches appear there. Never guess from raw source: `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO` etc. are defined in internal headers, not build.sh. For dispatch functions, also cross-check with `${CFG_WORK_DIR}/t_<test>_perf.objdump` `jal`/`jalr` targets to confirm which sub-kernel was linked.
641	- Focus on data flow, algorithm choice, tiling/blocking, loop structure, GEMM strategy, im2col, unrolling, LMUL selection, vector register utilization. Compute arithmetic intensity and reuse rate from the assembly. Must read `docs/wiki/index.md` and relevant wiki pages for what's been tried. Read `${CFG_UARCH_REF}` for pipeline/stall reasoning.
642	- Output: analysis summary including arithmetic intensity, reuse rate, active code path (verified from preprocessed `.i`, cross-checked with objdump for dispatch cases), and 1–3 ranked ideas with expected impact rationale, risk assessment, and which lines to modify
643	
644	**For `error-fix` subagent (on `RESULT: COMPILE_ERROR`):**
645	- Dispatch: `Agent(model="sonnet", ...)`. Prepend the prompt with `[effort: high]`.
646	- Tools allowed: Read, Grep, Glob, Edit
647	- DO NOT run any Bash commands except `grep` for checking macro availability in headers
648	- Input: the full gcc stderr output + the source file path of the file being optimized
649	- Read the error, identify which macro/function/type is missing or misused (often `__riscv_*` intrinsics used where config.toml marks `ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC` undefined), and edit the source to fix it. Same intrinsic rules as `implement`. Reference: `${CFG_INTRINSIC_API}`.
650	- Output: description of exact code change made to fix the error
651	
652	**For `implement` subagent:**
653	- Dispatch: `Agent(model="opus", ...)`. Prepend the prompt with `[effort: xhigh]`.
654	- Tools allowed: Read, Grep, Glob, Edit
655	- DO NOT run any Bash commands except `grep` for checking macro availability in headers
656	- DO NOT run build, test, QEMU, or FPGA commands
657	- DO NOT compile anything
658	- **CRITICAL: Use `NDS_VEC_*` macros ONLY** (e.g., `NDS_VEC_VSETVLI`, `NDS_VEC_VLE8_V`, `NDS_VEC_VQMACC_VX`). NEVER use `__riscv_*` C intrinsics — they require `ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC` which is undefined for this target (see `[target.active_defines].undefined` in config.toml). Read `${CFG_INTRINSIC_API}` first; then study an existing working function in the same `#ifdef ENA_VEC_ISA` block for live usage.
659	- Output: description of exact code change made
660	
661	---
662	
663	## Rules
664	
665	1. **Fully autonomous.** Do not wait for user input at any step. Run the entire loop.
666	2. **Main agent drives the FSM.** The main agent owns state, runs mechanical steps directly, dispatches reasoning steps to subagents, and reports progress to the user between every step.
667	3. **Never skip steps.** Follow FSM transitions strictly. `validate` runs before the first `select` of every session (cache-aware).
668	4. **Max `[limits].max_rounds` rounds per function** (5 by default). After that, exit to idle regardless.
669	5. **analyze is the gatekeeper.** If it finds no optimization opportunity from code + assembly + metrics, exit to idle. Do not force pointless changes.
670	6. **FPGA is ground truth.** QEMU is correctness only.
671	7. **Always record lessons.** Wins AND losses. This compounds knowledge across functions.
672	8. **Target VLEN = `[qemu].vlen` = `${CFG_QEMU_VLEN}`.** Never sacrifice primary-VLEN performance when tuning for secondary VLENs.
673	9. **If verify fails 3 times**, go back to `analyze` with a different approach, not the same one.
674	10. **If a round regresses, the auto-revert in `measure` already restored the working tree from the pre-implement checkpoint** (`refs/optimize-ckpt/<fn>/round-N-pre`). Do NOT manually `git checkout HEAD --` -- that would discard prior-session uncommitted gains. The next round starts from the LAST GOOD state automatically.
675	10a. **Checkpoint discipline.** `select` snapshots round-0 baseline as `refs/optimize-ckpt/<fn>/round-0-baseline`. `implement` step 0 snapshots the pre-implement state as `refs/optimize-ckpt/<fn>/round-N-pre` and records `checkpoint_ref` + `changed_files` in state.json. `measure` consults these on regression to revert atomically. Use `git for-each-ref refs/optimize-ckpt/` to inspect all checkpoints; `git diff <ref> -- <file>` to compare any round to current. NEVER use `git checkout HEAD -- <file>` or `git stash drop` inside the loop.
676	11. **Arithmetic intensity and reuse rate drive the next round.** Recompute them each round from updated assembly to track whether changes improved memory efficiency.
677	12. **Use `${SCRIPT_DIR}/rebuild_single.sh` for incremental builds** after the initial `build_libnn.sh` in `select`. Never rebuild the full library in the optimization loop.
678	13. **Use `${SCRIPT_DIR}/run_fpga_test.sh` for FPGA measurement.** It handles compilation with perf flags, GDB upload, and cycle count parsing. **NEVER compile or run FPGA tests manually — always use this script.**
679	14. **Only test the target function** during the optimization loop. Full regression is for final validation only.
680	15. **Scripts are the single source of truth for builds and tests.** Never construct gcc, QEMU, or GDB commands manually. Always use the scripts in `.claude/skills/optimize/script/`.
681	16. **No hardcoded target values.** Target CPU, toolchain, VLEN, CFLAGS, FPGA host, and active-defines all live in `config.toml`. SKILL.md and scripts never embed them literally.
682	17. **Emit a trace every reasoning step.** After `analyze`, `implement`, `verify`, `measure`, and `learn`, call `${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh step <step_name> <payload>` (see Trace Emission section). On any verify/measure failure path, also call `emit_trace.sh fail <tag> <stderr>`. Timing is automatic via the state-transition block; do not emit timing by hand. Trace emission is additive and MUST NOT alter FSM transitions.
683

file.content:
---
name: optimize
description: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. Target VPU, toolchain, build flags, FPGA board, and hardware profile all come from config.toml — no hardcoded paths or CPU names. Runs a 7-step FSM — validate, select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
argument-hint: [validate|function_name|status|reset]
disable-model-invocation: false
allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash, Edit, Write, Agent
---

# libnn RVV Optimization Skill

Fully autonomous optimization loop. Target (VPU, toolchain, FPGA, CFLAGS) is configured in `.claude/skills/optimize/config.toml`. State persisted in the `state_file` path set there (default: `.claude/skills/optimize/state.json`).

## Current State

!`LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel); SKILL_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize"; source "${SKILL_DIR}/script/parse_config.sh" 2>/dev/null; cat "${CFG_STATE_FILE:-${SKILL_DIR}/state.json}" 2>/dev/null || echo '{"step":"idle","function":"","round":0,"history":[]}'`

## Commands

- `/optimize validate` — Run config.toml validation (Step 0) only
- `/optimize <function_name>` — Start optimizing a function (validates first, then loops until flat land)
- `/optimize status` — Show current state
- `/optimize reset` — Reset to idle

Arguments received: `$ARGUMENTS`

## Finite State Machine

```
idle → validate → select → analyze ──→ implement → verify ──→ measure ──→ learn ─┐
          |         ↑  |         ↑          |  |                                  |
          |         |  |         └──────────┘  |                                  |
          |         |  |      (test fail → fix)|                                  |
          |         |  |                       └→ analyze                         |
          |         |  |                (3 retries exhausted)                     |
          |         |  └→ idle (no opportunity found)                             |
          |         |                                                             |
          |         └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
          |           (loop with new insights + assembly analysis)
          └→ idle (config validation failed)

Exit conditions:
  - validate fails → idle (fix config.toml, retry)
  - analyze finds no optimization opportunity → idle
  - max [limits].max_rounds per function → idle (5 by default; see config.toml)
```

The loop is the core mechanic: after `learn`, go back to `analyze` with fresh insights from FPGA measurements, updated assembly, and recomputed metrics. The verify/measure loop provides real feedback.

---

## Build Scripts Reference

All scripts live under the skill itself at `.claude/skills/optimize/script/`. They each source `parse_config.sh` to pull toolchain paths, CFLAGS, QEMU/FPGA targets, and work dir from `config.toml` — no hardcoded values. Always invoke with `/usr/bin/bash`.

**Shell-variable convention for every bash block in this skill:**
```bash
LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
```
When you need config values (work dir, toolchain, etc.) inside a bash block, `source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh"` and use the exported `CFG_*` variables (e.g. `${CFG_WORK_DIR}`, `${CFG_CC}`, `${CFG_CPU}`).

| Script | Purpose | Time |
|--------|---------|------|
| `validate_config.py` | 7-step fail-fast config validation (toolchain, QEMU, FPGA .inc parse, FPGA TCP, GDB run). Used by `validate` state. | ~10s |
| `build_libnn.sh` | Full rebuild of libnn.a (~400 files) using `${CFG_CPU}`. Use ONLY for initial build in `select` step. | ~30s |
| `rebuild_single.sh <src.c>` | Recompile single .c with `${CFG_CFLAGS_BASE}`, update libnn.a + objdump. Use for ALL rebuilds after `implement`. | ~0.6s |
| `rebuild_single.sh -E <src.c>` | Preprocess only (same CFLAGS). Output: `${CFG_WORK_DIR}/<basename>.i`. Use in `analyze` to see which `#ifdef` branches survive. | ~0.3s |
| `run_qemu_test.sh <name>` | Build test binary + run on QEMU with fd-9 strace capture + parse pass/fail. Uses `${CFG_QEMU_*}`. Use in `verify`. | ~2s |
| `run_fpga_test.sh <name>` | FPGA cycle count measurement via GDB. Uses `${CFG_FPGA_*}`. Use in `select` baseline + `measure`. | ~30s |
| `emit_trace.sh <sub>`    | Structured trace capture (Phase 0 meta-harness). Subcommands: `init`, `step`, `timing`, `fail`. Writes artifacts under `trace_corpus/<fn>/round_<N>/`. Auto-reads fn/round from state.json. | <1s |

---

## Trace Emission (Phase 0 meta-harness)

After each reasoning step produces output, the main agent writes a structured
trace artifact to `trace_corpus/<fn>/round_<N>/` via `${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh`.
These artifacts are the input to the outer meta-loop's harness-critic (future phase).
Trace emission does NOT change FSM behavior.

**Per-step artifacts:**

| Step | Artifact | Contents |
|------|----------|----------|
| analyze   | `analyze_agent.md`    | Subagent hypothesis, ranked ideas, AI/reuse metrics |
| implement | `implement_agent.md`  | Subagent's description of the exact code change |
| verify    | `verify_result.json`  | `{"result": "PASS\|FAIL\|COMPILE_ERROR\|TIMEOUT\|UNKNOWN", "sub_tests": N, "stderr": "..."}` |
| measure   | `measure_result.json` | `{"cycles": N, "instrs": M, "ipc": X, "speedup": Y}` |
| learn     | `learn_agent.md`      | Lessons written to wiki this round (summary) |
| (any)     | `fail_reason.md`      | Tagged failure class: `compile_error`, `test_fail`, `regression`, `timeout`, `unknown` |

Timing (wall-clock per step) is appended to `step_timings.json` automatically by
the state-transition block — no explicit call needed.

**Emission pattern** (auto-mode reads fn/round from state.json; no flags needed):

```bash
LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
# Write payload to a temp file, then emit:
cat > /tmp/trace_payload <<'EOF'
<content>
EOF
/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh step <step_name> /tmp/trace_payload
# Or on failure:
/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh fail <tag> /tmp/error_stderr
```

No trace is emitted from `validate` or `select` — trace lifecycle begins at
`analyze` (which runs in a round context).

---

## State: `idle`

No optimization in progress.

Routing:
- `/optimize validate` → `validate` (run 7-step check, then return to `idle`)
- `/optimize <function_name>` → `validate` first (cache-aware); on PASS, continue to `select`; on FAIL, stay `idle`
- `/optimize status` → report current state, stay `idle`
- `/optimize reset` → clear state, stay `idle`

---

## State: `validate`

Check that `config.toml` points at a working toolchain, reachable QEMU, parseable FPGA .inc, and live FPGA board BEFORE any optimization work starts. Failing fast here saves a lot of pain later.

1. Cache check (skip re-validation if nothing has changed):
   ```bash
   LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
   SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
   source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh"
   # Skip if last validated within [limits].validate_cache_hours AND
   # config.toml + .inc mtimes are not newer than state.validated_at
   ```
   If cached validation is still fresh, skip to step 4.

2. Run the 7-step validator:
   ```bash
   python3 "${SCRIPT_DIR}/validate_config.py"
   ```
   The script prints `[OK]` / `[FAIL]` per step and exits non-zero on the first failure. The 7 steps are:
   1. Load `config.toml` — all required sections/keys present
   2. Toolchain — `${CFG_TOOLCHAIN_BIN}` exists, `${CFG_CC}` accepts Andes flags
   3. Compile hello world — produces valid RISC-V ELF
   4. QEMU — `${CFG_QEMU_BIN}` runs hello.adx (strace-captured semihosting)
   5. FPGA .inc parse — `${CFG_FPGA_CONFIG}` parses (VLEN, caches, ISA features)
   6. FPGA TCP — connect to `${CFG_FPGA_HOST}:${CFG_FPGA_PORT}` (5s timeout)
   7. FPGA GDB run — compile hello_fpga and run it on the board

3. On `[FAIL]`:
   - Report which step failed and what needs fixing in `config.toml` (or auxiliary files like the .inc path).
   - Steps 6 / 7 may fail if the FPGA board is unreachable from the current network. When that happens and the user only needs QEMU-only work, they can skip by setting `[limits].validate_cache_hours` high after a prior success, or by running `/optimize validate` from a network with FPGA access.
   - Transition → `idle`. Do NOT proceed to `select`.

4. On `[OK]` (or cache hit): update state:
   ```json
   {"validated_at": "<ISO timestamp>", "config_toml_mtime": <stat mtime>}
   ```
   If `/optimize validate` was called directly → transition → `idle` with a success summary.
   If `/optimize <function>` triggered validation → transition → `select`.

**Transition:** → `select` (when invoked via function-name path) or → `idle`.

---

## State: `select`

Validate the function and gather baseline context.

1. Find the source file under `Source/` — grep for the function name
2. Confirm it has `#ifdef ENA_VEC_ISA` (existing RVV to improve)
3. Find corresponding test in `Examples/unit_func/t_<name>.c`
4. Query the wiki for prior lessons and applicable techniques:
   - Read `docs/wiki/index.md` to identify relevant pages
   - Read the operator page in `docs/wiki/operators/` if it exists (e.g., `conv-hwc-s8-sym-bias-fast.md`)
   - Read matching `docs/wiki/general/` pages for applicable techniques and known anti-patterns
5. Full build (first time only):
   ```bash
   /usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/build_libnn.sh
   ```
6. Run baseline FPGA measurement (**MUST use this exact script — do NOT compile or run FPGA tests manually**):
   ```bash
   /usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/run_fpga_test.sh <test_name>
   ```
   Parse both counts from the output lines (same format as `measure` state):
   - `"The cycle count is <N>"` → `cycles_before`
   - `"The inst count is <N>"` → `instrs_before`
   (The `RESULT: PASS (cycles=N)` summary line also carries cycles but not instrs, so use the raw lines for both.)
7. **Identify the active code path.** Primary: preprocess the source with the same build flags and read the surviving `#ifdef` branches:
   ```bash
   /usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/rebuild_single.sh -E <source_file>
   # Output: ${CFG_WORK_DIR}/<basename>.i
   ```
   Secondary (for dispatch functions that call sub-kernels): confirm the linked sub-function names from the perf objdump:
   ```bash
   grep -A500 '<function_name>:' ${CFG_WORK_DIR}/t_<test>_perf.objdump | \
     grep -E "(jal|jalr).*<[^+>]+>" | head -20
   ```
   Save `active_path_file` (path to `.i`) and `called_functions` in state.
8. Save state: function, source_file, test_name, cycles_before, instrs_before, active_path_file, called_functions.
9. **Capture round-0 baseline checkpoint.** Snapshot the current working tree (with any prior-session uncommitted edits) so any future round can restore to this clean baseline:
   ```bash
   LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
   FN=<function_name>
   REF="refs/optimize-ckpt/${FN}/round-0-baseline"
   SHA=$(cd "${LIB_ROOT}" && git stash create "optimize ${FN} round-0 baseline")
   if [ -n "${SHA}" ]; then
     git -C "${LIB_ROOT}" update-ref "${REF}" "${SHA}"
   else
     git -C "${LIB_ROOT}" update-ref "${REF}" HEAD
   fi
   # Persist in state for diagnostics
   python3 - "${CFG_STATE_FILE}" "${REF}" <<'PYEOF'
   import json,sys
   f, ref = sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2]
   s = json.load(open(f))
   s['baseline_ref'] = ref
   json.dump(s, open(f,'w'), indent=2)
   PYEOF
   ```
   These refs live under `refs/optimize-ckpt/<fn>/...` — invisible to `git branch` / `git tag`, won't pollute history, but are inspectable via `git for-each-ref refs/optimize-ckpt/`.

**Transition:** → `analyze`
**Fail:** Function not found or no RVV → stay `select`, report error.

---

## State: `analyze`

Study the current RVV code and assembly to form the next optimization hypothesis.

**CRITICAL — Identify the active code path from preprocessed source, not from raw `#ifdef` guards.**

Many `#ifdef` guards depend on macros defined in internal headers (e.g., `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO` are unconditionally defined in `internal/internal_nn_math.h`), NOT just build.sh flags. **Never guess the active path from raw source alone.** Instead:

1. **Primary method — preprocessed source.** Run the file through the preprocessor with the exact build flags:
   ```bash
   /usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/rebuild_single.sh -E <source_file.c>
   # Output: ${CFG_WORK_DIR}/<basename>.i
   ```
   The `.i` file contains only the branches that survive preprocessing — it directly answers "which `#ifdef` block is active" without inference. Grep for the function in the `.i` file and read that body. This works even when the active block is fully inline (no sub-function calls), which objdump cannot disambiguate.

2. **Secondary method — objdump cross-check.** For functions that dispatch to sub-kernels, confirm by checking which sub-functions the linked binary actually calls:
   ```bash
   grep -E "(jal|jalr).*<[^+>]+>" ${CFG_WORK_DIR}/t_<test>_perf.objdump | grep -v "gdb\|nds\|start\|__" | head -20
   ```
   Cross-reference call targets (e.g., `nn_mat_mul_kernel_tiling_q7` vs `nn_mat_mul_kernel_q7_bias_2sft`) with the preprocessed source. This catches cases where LTO/dead-code elimination pruned a branch that `-E` still shows.

3. When mapping the `.i` body back to raw source for editing, target only the `#ifdef` branch whose body matches the `.i`. Modifying any other branch has zero effect.

**Active-defines hint.** For a quick list of which macros `config.toml` declares defined vs undefined, source `parse_config.sh` and inspect `${CFG_DEFINES_DEFINED}` and `${CFG_DEFINES_UNDEFINED}` (from `[target.active_defines]`). These are a hint only — the `.i` file is always authoritative, because internal headers can redefine things without any config.toml knowledge.

### Analysis

**On first round (round=1):**
1. Identify the active code path from the `.i` file saved in `active_path_file` by `select`. Read the function body there to understand what will actually execute.
2. Find the function's assembly in `libnn.objdump` (regenerated by `rebuild_single.sh` after every compile; located at `${CFG_LIBNN_ROOT}/libnn.objdump`):
   ```bash
   grep -A 200 '<function_name>:' ${CFG_LIBNN_ROOT}/libnn.objdump | head -300
   ```
3. If the function calls sub-functions (GEMM kernels, etc.), run `${SCRIPT_DIR}/rebuild_single.sh -E` on each sub-function's source file too, and read its assembly from `libnn.objdump`. Sub-functions have their own `#ifdef` branches.
4. Query the wiki: read `docs/wiki/index.md`, relevant `general/` pages for similar patterns, operator page if it exists
5. Compute key metrics from the assembly and source:
   - **Arithmetic Intensity** = FLOPs per byte transferred from memory. Count MAC-equivalent ops (e.g., each `vqmacc` contributes `2 * vl` FLOPs for LMUL-dependent `vl`); count bytes loaded/stored per loop iteration (load instructions x element width x vl). AI = FLOPs / bytes.
   - **Reuse Rate** = FLOPs per byte loaded (exclude stores). Measures how many times each loaded byte contributes to computation before being evicted. High reuse = cache-friendly; low reuse = memory-bound.
   - Save both as `arith_intensity` and `reuse_rate` in state.
6. Identify bottleneck using the metrics: low AI -> memory-bound (improve tiling/blocking/reuse); high AI but low IPC -> compute-bound (LMUL, register pressure, loop structure)
7. Form hypothesis: what to change and expected impact

**On subsequent rounds (round>1):**
1. Re-run `${SCRIPT_DIR}/rebuild_single.sh -E <source_file>` to refresh the `.i` (the previous round's `implement` modified the source, and if any macros were touched the `#ifdef` resolution may have shifted). Re-read the active block.
2. Re-check `${CFG_LIBNN_ROOT}/libnn.objdump` for the updated assembly of the function.
3. Recompute arithmetic intensity and reuse rate from the updated assembly. Compare to previous round — did the change improve reuse or reduce memory traffic?
4. Review FPGA cycle/instruction delta from last round's `measure` step to identify new bottleneck
5. Query the wiki: read `docs/wiki/index.md` and relevant `general/` pages for the specific bottleneck pattern
6. Form the next hypothesis based on what the metrics reveal

**Proposer output:** 1–3 ranked ideas, each with what to change, which lines, expected impact rationale, and risk assessment. Include current arithmetic intensity and reuse rate in the summary. Pick the top idea as the hypothesis.

### Output and Transitions

**Output:** Print the analysis summary (including arithmetic intensity, reuse rate, and hypothesis). Do NOT wait for approval — proceed immediately.

**Trace emit:** Write the subagent's full analysis (hypothesis, AI, reuse rate, ranked ideas, risk) to a tmp file, then:
```bash
/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh step analyze /tmp/analyze_trace.md
```

**Decision gate:** If no concrete optimization opportunity can be identified (assembly looks clean, metrics are already optimal, LMUL/tiling are already well-tuned):
- Transition to idle with final summary.
- Do NOT force an optimization that has no clear rationale.

**Transition:** → `implement` (if opportunity found) or → `idle` (if no opportunity)

---

## State: `implement`

Apply the optimization.

**Step 0 — capture a checkpoint of the working tree BEFORE any edit.** This is the rollback point if the round regresses. Uses `git stash create` (snapshot blob; does NOT modify the working tree, index, or branch state) plus `git update-ref` (anchors the blob so git GC won't collect it). One ref per round per function.

```bash
LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh"
FN=$(python3 -c "import json;print(json.load(open('${CFG_STATE_FILE}'))['function'])")
ROUND=$(python3 -c "import json;print(json.load(open('${CFG_STATE_FILE}'))['round'])")
REF="refs/optimize-ckpt/${FN}/round-${ROUND}-pre"
SHA=$(cd "${LIB_ROOT}" && git stash create "optimize ${FN} round-${ROUND} pre-implement")
if [ -n "${SHA}" ]; then
  git -C "${LIB_ROOT}" update-ref "${REF}" "${SHA}"
else
  # No tracked-file changes vs HEAD: anchor HEAD itself so revert works
  git -C "${LIB_ROOT}" update-ref "${REF}" HEAD
fi
# Persist the ref name in state for the measure step's auto-revert
python3 - "${CFG_STATE_FILE}" "${REF}" <<'PYEOF'
import json,sys
f, ref = sys.argv[1], sys.argv[2]
s = json.load(open(f))
s['checkpoint_ref'] = ref
json.dump(s, open(f,'w'), indent=2)
PYEOF
```

Then perform the edit:

1. Modify ONLY the active code path identified during `analyze`. The active path was determined from the preprocessed `.i` file (with objdump cross-check for dispatch functions). **Do NOT guess from `#ifdef` guards in the raw source** — many macros (e.g., `ENA_TILING`, `ENA_FAST_ALGO`) are defined in internal headers, not build.sh. Map from the `.i` body back to the corresponding raw-source line numbers when editing.
2. If the function calls sub-functions (e.g., a GEMM kernel), the optimization may need to be applied in the sub-function's source file, not the conv function's source.
3. Do NOT touch the plain C fallback or dead `#ifdef` blocks
4. Save a description of what was changed in state (`changes` field), AND list the modified files in state under `changed_files: [...]` so `measure` knows what to revert on regression.
5. If this is round>1 and the previous round regressed, the prior round's `measure` step has ALREADY auto-reverted via the checkpoint; the working tree is back to the pre-regression state. Just proceed with the new approach.

**CRITICAL — Intrinsic API selection:**
The active `ENA_VEC_ISA` code path uses **`NDS_VEC_*` macros** (e.g., `NDS_VEC_VSETVLI`, `NDS_VEC_VLE8_V`, `NDS_VEC_VQMACC_VX`, `NDS_VEC_VNCLIP_WX`). These are defined in `internal/internal_vec_isa_v*.h` (version chosen by `NDS_VEC_RVV_VERSION` in `[target.cflags_base]`).

**NEVER use `__riscv_*` C intrinsics** (e.g., `__riscv_vle8_v_i8m2`, `__riscv_vqmacc_vx_i32m8`) in `ENA_VEC_ISA` blocks. Those require `ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC`, which is listed under `[target.active_defines].undefined` in `config.toml` for this target. Using them causes compilation failures.

The canonical reference for legal macros is `${CFG_INTRINSIC_API}` (path from `[target].intrinsic_api`). Read that file before writing new RVV code. In addition:
- Study an existing working function in the same `#ifdef` block to see live usage patterns
- Use explicit register names (`NDS_VEC_V0`, `NDS_VEC_V8`, `NDS_VEC_V16`, etc.)
- Match the LMUL register grouping: M8 uses v0/v8/v16/v24, M4 uses v0/v4/v8/.., M2 uses v0/v2/v4/..
- For loads: `NDS_VEC_VLE8_V` (e8), `NDS_VEC_VLE16_V` (e16), `NDS_VEC_VLE32_V` (e32)
- For stores: `NDS_VEC_VSE8_V` (e8), `NDS_VEC_VSE16_V` (e16), `NDS_VEC_VSE32_V` (e32)

**Trace emit:** After the implement subagent returns, write its description of the exact change (which file, which lines, what replaced what, reasoning) to a tmp file, then:
```bash
/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh step implement /tmp/implement_trace.md
```

**Transition:** → `verify`

---

## State: `verify`

Incremental rebuild and QEMU correctness test — all via scripts.

1. Rebuild ONLY the modified libnn source (fast incremental):
   ```bash
   /usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/rebuild_single.sh <source_file>
   ```

2. Build the test binary AND run it on QEMU with fd-9 semihosting capture in one script call:
   ```bash
   /usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/run_qemu_test.sh <test_name>
   ```
   The script reads `${CFG_QEMU_*}` + `${CFG_CFLAGS_BASE}` + `${CFG_CFLAGS_VERIFY}` from `config.toml`, compiles `Examples/unit_func/t_<test_name>.c` against `libnn.a`, runs under `strace -e trace=write` to capture fd-9 semihosting, and emits one of:
   - `RESULT: PASS (N sub-tests passed)` — correctness verified
   - `RESULT: FAIL (N failures detected)` — compiled but wrong results
   - `RESULT: COMPILE_ERROR` — gcc failed; full error is on stderr
   - `RESULT: TIMEOUT (Ns)` — QEMU hang
   - `RESULT: UNKNOWN (...)` — no pass/fail marker in output

3. Do NOT run full regression in the optimization loop — only the target function's test.

**Trace emit:** After `run_qemu_test.sh` returns, capture the result to a JSON payload (one-shot, regardless of pass/fail):
```bash
cat > /tmp/verify_trace.json <<EOF
{"result": "PASS|FAIL|COMPILE_ERROR|TIMEOUT|UNKNOWN", "sub_tests": <N>, "stderr": "<first few lines if any>"}
EOF
/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh step verify /tmp/verify_trace.json
```
On non-PASS also emit a failure tag (writes `fail_reason.md`). Tag mapping:
- `COMPILE_ERROR` → `compile_error` (pass gcc stderr as message file)
- `FAIL` → `test_fail`
- `TIMEOUT` → `timeout`
- `UNKNOWN` → `unknown`
```bash
/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh fail <tag> /tmp/qemu_stderr.txt
```

**Pass:** → `measure`
**`COMPILE_ERROR`:** Launch ONE `error-fix` subagent with the full stderr + source file path. It reads the error and edits the source. Re-run step 1+2. Max 3 compile-fix retries before going back to `analyze`.
**`FAIL` (wrong results):** → `implement` (diagnose + fix). Max 3 test-fix retries before going back to `analyze`.
**`TIMEOUT` / `UNKNOWN`:** Treat as FAIL, same retry policy.

---

## State: `measure`

Run on the FPGA board via GDB for ground-truth cycle counts.

```bash
/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/run_fpga_test.sh <test_name>
```

This script reads `[target].cpu`, `[target.cflags_base]`, `[target.cflags_perf]`, `[fpga].*` from `config.toml` and:
1. Compiles the test with `${CFG_CFLAGS_BASE} ${CFG_CFLAGS_PERF}` (includes `-DPF_COUNTER -DENA_TEST_PERF -DENA_RUN_TWICE` on the perf list)
2. Uploads binary to FPGA via the GDB expect script at `${CFG_FPGA_SIM_WRAPPER}`
3. Runs on the board at `${CFG_FPGA_HOST}:${CFG_FPGA_PORT}`
4. Generates `.adx` and `.objdump` in `${CFG_WORK_DIR}/`

Parse the output for:
- `"The cycle count is <N>"` — FPGA cycle count
- `"The inst count is <N>"` — FPGA instruction count
- `"accuracy checking pass/fail"` — correctness check

Save `cycles_after` and `instrs_after` in state. Compute speedup: `cycles_before / cycles_after`. Compute IPC: `instrs_after / cycles_after`.

**Trace emit:** After parsing, write a JSON payload and emit:
```bash
cat > /tmp/measure_trace.json <<EOF
{"cycles": <cycles_after>, "instrs": <instrs_after>, "ipc": <ipc>, "speedup": <speedup>, "cycles_before": <cycles_before>}
EOF
/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh step measure /tmp/measure_trace.json
```
If speedup < 1.0 (regression), also emit a failure tag AND **auto-restore the working tree from the pre-implement checkpoint captured in `implement` step 0**:
```bash
echo "cycles regressed: ${cycles_before} -> ${cycles_after} (speedup ${speedup})" > /tmp/regression.txt
/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh fail regression /tmp/regression.txt

# AUTO-REVERT from checkpoint
LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh"
REF=$(python3 -c "import json;print(json.load(open('${CFG_STATE_FILE}'))['checkpoint_ref'])")
FILES=$(python3 -c "import json;print(' '.join(json.load(open('${CFG_STATE_FILE}')).get('changed_files',[])))")
if [ -n "${REF}" ] && [ -n "${FILES}" ]; then
  git -C "${LIB_ROOT}" checkout "${REF}" -- ${FILES}
  /usr/bin/bash "${SCRIPT_DIR}/rebuild_single.sh" ${FILES}  # rebuild from restored source
  echo "Auto-reverted ${FILES} from ${REF}; libnn.a is back to pre-round-${ROUND} state."
else
  echo "WARN: no checkpoint_ref or changed_files in state — manual revert needed."
fi
```

**Transition:** → `learn`

---

## State: `learn`

Record the lesson, then loop back.

1. Update the wiki following the wiki-writer skill:
   - Read `.claude/skills/wiki-writer/SKILL.md`
   - Input context from state: function, source_file, round, hypothesis, changes, cycles_before, cycles_after, speedup, arith_intensity, reuse_rate, outcome
   - Execute all 4 operations: operator page, general page (if applicable), index, log
   - All content must follow the naive expression style defined in wiki-writer

2. **Trace emit** (before the round counter bumps, so attribution stays on this round):
   ```bash
   cat > /tmp/learn_trace.md <<EOF
   <1-3 sentence summary of lessons recorded this round; cite which wiki pages updated>
   EOF
   /usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/emit_trace.sh step learn /tmp/learn_trace.md
   ```

3. **Commit the round atomically.** A round whose `verify` PASSED and whose `measure` was not a regression (`speedup >= 1.0`) is "good enough to keep" -- commit it so the git history mirrors `round_history`, and the next round starts from a clean working tree. The commit covers ONLY the files listed in `state.changed_files` (the perf-relevant edits) -- never `git add -A` (that would drag in skill state, work-dir artifacts, wiki edits, etc.; wiki commits happen separately if at all). Regressed rounds were already reverted in `measure`, so there is nothing to commit -- skip this step.
   ```bash
   LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
   SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
   source "${SCRIPT_DIR}/parse_config.sh"
   # Source state into shell vars (python writes a sourceable file -- safe across
   # multi-word approach strings, paths with spaces, etc.).
   python3 - "${CFG_STATE_FILE}" > /tmp/optimize_commit_env <<'PYEOF'
import json,sys,shlex
s=json.load(open(sys.argv[1]))
last=s['round_history'][-1] if s.get('round_history') else {}
def emit(k,v): print(f"{k}={shlex.quote(str(v))}")
emit('FN',       s.get('function',''))
emit('ROUND',    s.get('round',''))
emit('APPROACH', last.get('approach',''))
emit('CYC_BEFORE', s.get('cycles_before',''))
emit('CYC_AFTER',  s.get('cycles_after',''))
emit('SPEEDUP',  s.get('speedup',''))
emit('OUTCOME',  last.get('outcome',''))
emit('FILES',    ' '.join(s.get('changed_files',[])))
PYEOF
   . /tmp/optimize_commit_env
   if [ "${OUTCOME}" = "improved" ] || [ "${OUTCOME}" = "neutral" ]; then
     if [ -n "${FILES}" ]; then
       git -C "${LIB_ROOT}" add -- ${FILES}
       git -C "${LIB_ROOT}" commit -m "$(cat <<COMMIT
[opt/rvv] ${FN}: round ${ROUND} -- ${APPROACH}

Cycles ${CYC_BEFORE} -> ${CYC_AFTER} (${SPEEDUP}x). Outcome: ${OUTCOME}.
Test: t_${FN} 3/3 sub-tests pass on FPGA-measured binary.

Co-Authored-By: optimize-skill <noreply@anthropic.com>
COMMIT
)" || echo "WARN: commit failed (likely no staged changes -- e.g. asm-only reorder elided by compiler, or files already committed)"
     else
       echo "WARN: no changed_files in state -- skipping commit"
     fi
   else
     echo "Skipping commit: outcome=${OUTCOME} (regression was reverted in measure step)"
