attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. Target VPU, toolchain, build flags, FPGA board, and hardware profile all come from config.toml — no hardcoded paths or CPU names. Runs a 7-step FSM — validate, select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
- hot-inner-kernel-extractor: Extract a libnn function's hot inner-most loop into a standalone, mcycle-wrapped RISC-V assembly harness so it can be measured on a cycle-accurate Verilator simulator (or any cycle counter) without running the whole function. Use this skill whenever the user wants to study pipeline behavior of a libnn kernel, profile inner-loop cycles, build a microbenchmark from an existing function, prepare a Verilator workload, isolate a hot loop body, or asks about "extracting", "isolating", "harnessing", "microbenchmark", "Verilator timeview", "pipeline timeview", "steady state", or "inner kernel" of any libnn / RVV function. The skill identifies the hottest translation block via QEMU's hotblocks plugin, walks objdump to find the innermost backward branch, computes live-in registers, and emits a compilable .S harness that runs the body N times under csrr mcycle.
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Compile succeeded. The FPGA board at `sw-boards.andestech.com:1113` reset the connection:

```
Remote debugging using sw-boards.andestech.com:1113
Remote communication error. Target disconnected: error while reading: Connection reset by peer.
Couldn't set target for remote stub.
```

This is a board-side or network issue -- could be another user holding the board, or the board needing a power-cycle / `First_Reset_done` cleared. The binary is at `${CFG_WORK_DIR}/t_conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any_perf.adx` and ready to run when the board is reachable.

Try again, or check whether the board is in use? Want me to retry?

message.content: how to run QEMU for correctness?

content.text:
## QEMU correctness check

```bash
LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/run_qemu_test.sh conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any --strace
```

## Flags

| Flag | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `--strace` | **Always pass this in Claude Code.** Andes QEMU writes semihosting output to fd 9, not stdout; without strace the test reports UNKNOWN. |
| `--timeout N` | Override `[qemu].timeout` (default 300s) |
| `--config <path>` | Use a non-default `config.toml` |

## What it does

1. Compiles `Examples/unit_func/t_<test_name>.c` against `${CFG_LIBNN_ROOT}/libnn.a` using `${CFG_CFLAGS_BASE} ${CFG_CFLAGS_VERIFY}` (verify flags, no PF_COUNTER).
2. Runs the resulting `.adx` under `${CFG_QEMU_BIN}` with `-M ${CFG_QEMU_MACHINE} -cpu ${CFG_QEMU_CPU}` and the device config from `${CFG_QEMU_CONFIG}`.
3. Wraps QEMU in `strace -f -e trace=write` -> `${CFG_WORK_DIR}/strace_<name>.log`.
4. Greps `write(9, ...)` for `accuracy checking pass/fail`.

## Result lines

| Line | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| `RESULT: PASS (N sub-tests passed)` | all sub-tests checked |
| `RESULT: FAIL (N failures detected)` | compiled, ran, but wrong outputs |
| `RESULT: COMPILE_ERROR` | gcc failed (full error on stderr) |
| `RESULT: TIMEOUT (Ns)` | QEMU hang |
| `RESULT: UNKNOWN (...)` | no pass/fail markers; usually a missing `--strace` |

## Typical usage

After `rebuild_single.sh`, before `run_fpga_test.sh`:

```bash
/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/rebuild_single.sh Source/.../riscv_nn_conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any.c
/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/run_qemu_test.sh conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any --strace
/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/run_fpga_test.sh conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any
```

QEMU verifies correctness only -- never trust its cycle counts. FPGA is ground truth for cycles.

lastPrompt: how to run QEMU for correctness?

message.content: so, you compile executable twice?  any difference between QEMU and FPGA running?

content.text:
Yes, two separate compiles -- different CFLAGS bake in different code paths. Same `libnn.a` underneath; only the test ELF changes.

## Differences

| Dimension | QEMU (`run_qemu_test.sh`) | FPGA (`run_fpga_test.sh`) |
|---|---|---|
| Source | `Examples/unit_func/t_<name>.c` | `Examples/unit_func/t_<name>.c` (same) |
| Extra CFLAGS | `${CFG_CFLAGS_VERIFY}` -> `-DENA_FAST_ALGO -DENA_DUMP_GOLDEN=0 -DENA_MEASURE_PERF=0 -mext-vector=zve64d -mvh` | `${CFG_CFLAGS_PERF}` -> `-DPF_COUNTER -DENA_TEST_PERF -DENA_MEASURE_PERF=1 -DENA_RUN_TWICE -DENA_CACHEABLE_ENV_SETUP -mvh` |
| `PF_COUNTER` | **off** -- no cycle/inst CSR reads | **on** -- wraps the call in `startPFM`/`stopPFM` |
| `ENA_RUN_TWICE` | off -- single call per test | on -- runs the function twice; reports the 2nd run (warm-cache) |
| Linker script | none (uses QEMU's default ae350 layout) | `${CFG_FPGA_LD_SCRIPT}` = `project/ax45mpv/ae350-xip_hvm.ld` |
| Output | `${CFG_WORK_DIR}/t_<name>.adx` | `${CFG_WORK_DIR}/t_<name>_perf.adx` (note `_perf` suffix) |
| Runner | QEMU emulator + strace fd-9 | GDB expect script -> physical FPGA at `${CFG_FPGA_HOST}:${CFG_FPGA_PORT}` |
| Output channel | semihosting writes to fd 9 (captured via strace) | semihosting via the GDB session, tee'd to log |
| What it checks | `accuracy checking pass/fail` only | cycles, inst count, AND accuracy |
| Wall time | ~2 s | ~30 s (board upload + run + GDB chatter) |
| Trustworthy for | correctness | correctness + cycle counts (ground truth) |

## Why two binaries

- The verify ELF excludes `PF_COUNTER` so QEMU doesn't choke on `csrr mcycle`/`minstret` (Andes pfcounter macros use HW counters that vanilla QEMU doesn't model accurately) and so the test runs faster.
- The perf ELF needs `-DENA_RUN_TWICE` because the first call pays cold-cache + first-touch costs; only the second call's count goes in the report.
- The perf ELF uses the FPGA-specific linker script (cacheable XIP layout) so the binary actually maps to FPGA SRAM/flash addresses.

## Same library, different test wrappers

Both compiles link against the **same** `${CFG_LIBNN_ROOT}/libnn.a` -- the optimization you make in `Source/...` is in there once, and you just rebuild the wrapper test ELF twice. So `rebuild_single.sh` runs once per source edit; the two test scripts each do their own incremental gcc on the test source.

message.content: show me script command to run on both targets

content.text:
## Both targets, one block

```bash
LIB_ROOT=$(git rev-parse --show-toplevel)
SCRIPT_DIR="${LIB_ROOT}/.claude/skills/optimize/script"
FN=conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any
SRC=Source/ConvolutionFunctions/riscv_nn_conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any.c

/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/rebuild_single.sh ${SRC} \
  && /usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/run_qemu_test.sh ${FN} --strace \
  && /usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/run_fpga_test.sh ${FN}
```

`&&` short-circuits: if QEMU FAILs, FPGA is skipped (no point measuring cycles on broken code).

## Independently

```bash
# Correctness only (~2s)
/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/run_qemu_test.sh conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any --strace

# Cycle count only (~30s, board must be reachable)
/usr/bin/bash ${SCRIPT_DIR}/run_fpga_test.sh conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any
```

Both take the **bare function name** (no `t_` prefix, no `.c`) -- they prepend `t_` and look in `Examples/unit_func/`.

message.content: compile for running on QEMU then read /local/nick/qemu_v5/docs/hotloops.md to detect hot loops

content.content:
1	# hotloops - hot inner-loop detector
2	
3	Detects the hottest loops in a RISC-V ELF by observing backward
4	translation-block transitions in QEMU. For each detected loop you get
5	the loop header PC, the backward-branch PC, and the iteration count.
6	With DWARF, you also get the source `file:line`.
7	
8	Two parts ship together:
9	
10	- `contrib/plugins/hotloops.c` - QEMU TCG plugin that records edges.
11	- `scripts/hotloops.sh` - wrapper that runs the plugin and formats output.
12	
13	## Usage
14	
15	```
16	scripts/hotloops.sh [options] <elf> [--] [program args...]
17	```
18	
19	### Options
20	
21	| Flag                | Default | Description                                                    |
22	|---------------------|---------|----------------------------------------------------------------|
23	| `--arch <a>`        | auto    | Target arch: `riscv32`, `riscv64`, or `auto` (from `file`).    |
24	| `--qemu <path>`     | auto    | Override the QEMU binary.                                      |
25	| `--plugin <path>`   | auto    | Override the plugin `.so`.                                     |
26	| `--limit <N>`       | 50      | Print at most N loops.                                         |
27	| `--min-iters <N>`   | 1       | Drop loops with fewer than N iterations (hot-loop threshold).  |
28	| `--objdump <path>`  | auto    | Override objdump (for `function+offset` resolution).           |
29	| `--addr2line <path>`| auto    | Override addr2line (for source line resolution).               |
30	| `--no-source`       | off     | Skip source resolution even if DWARF is present.               |
31	| `--raw`             | off     | Print raw plugin output (CSV-ish), no formatting.              |
32	
33	### Build prerequisites
34	
35	The plugin lives in `contrib/plugins/`. Build once:
36	
37	```
38	ninja -C build contrib-plugins
39	```
40	
41	It is incremental on subsequent edits to `hotloops.c`. The QEMU binaries
42	themselves (`qemu-riscv64`, `qemu-riscv32`) do **not** need to be rebuilt.
43	
44	## How it detects a loop
45	
46	For every translated block, the plugin remembers the PC of its last
47	instruction. When the next-executed block starts at a PC `<=` that
48	remembered PC, the transition is a backward edge - i.e. a loop iteration.
49	The pair `(header_pc, tail_pc)` is the key; the count is the number of
50	iterations.
51	
52	This catches every loop a real backward branch closes (for/while/do-while,
53	goto, manually unrolled tails, etc.) without needing to model branch
54	semantics.
55	
56	Caveat: each iteration counted is one *taken* backward branch. A loop
57	that runs N times has N-1 taken backward branches plus one fall-through
58	on exit, so the reported count is N-1 per call to the enclosing function.
59	
60	## Output format
61	
62	Header line, then a fixed-width table sorted by `iters` descending:
63	
64	```
65	# hot loops (iters >= <threshold>), sorted desc, top <K>
66	iters | start_pc           -> end_pc             | location              | source
67	-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
68	<n>   | 0x<16-hex>         -> 0x<16-hex>         | <fn>+0x<o> -> <fn>+0x<o> | file:line -> file:line
69	...
70	```
71	
72	Columns:
73	
74	- `iters`     - taken backward-branch count (sorted desc).
75	- `start_pc`  - PC of the loop header (the branch target).
76	- `end_pc`    - PC of the backward branch (the loop tail).
77	- `location`  - `function+offset -> function+offset` from the ELF symbol table.
78	                Shows `?` if the address is not inside any sized FUNC symbol.
79	- `source`    - `file:line -> file:line` from DWARF. Collapsed to a single
80	                `file:line` when both ends map to the same source line.
81	                Only present when the ELF has debug info and `--no-source`
82	                is not set.
83	
84	The `--raw` mode prints the unformatted plugin output instead:
85	
86	```
87	hotloops: <distinct> distinct backward edges, <hot> hot (>= <thr> iters), sorted by iterations desc
88	start_pc, end_pc, iterations
89	0x..., 0x..., <n>
90	...
91	```
92	
93	## Example
94	
95	Source:
96	
97	```c
98	/* /tmp/hotloops_demo.c */
99	#include <stdio.h>

102	static int dot(volatile const int *a, volatile const int *b, int n) {
103	    int s = 0;

105	    return s;

109	    int a[64], b[64];
110	    for (int i = 0; i < 64; i++) { a[i] = i; b[i] = 64 - i; }
111	    long total = 0;
112	    for (int outer = 0; outer < 500; outer++)
113	        total += dot(a, b, 64);                      /* 64 * 500 inner iters */
114	    printf("total=%ld\n", total);
115	    return 0;
116	}
117	```
118	
119	Build with debug info and run:
120	
121	```
122	$ riscv64-linux-gnu-gcc -O1 -g -static /tmp/hotloops_demo.c -o /tmp/hotloops_demo
123	$ scripts/hotloops.sh --limit 5 --min-iters 100 /tmp/hotloops_demo
124	```
125	
126	Output:
127	
128	```
129	# hot loops (iters >= 100), sorted desc, top 5
130	iters | start_pc           -> end_pc             | location                                                                | source
131	-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
132	31500 | 0x000000000001056e -> 0x000000000001057c | dot+0xc -> dot+0x1a                                                     | hotloops_demo.c:6 (discriminator 3) -> hotloops_demo.c:6 (discriminator 1)
133	 1757 | 0x000000000004ab54 -> 0x000000000004abe8 | read_encoded_value_with_base+0x70 -> read_encoded_value_with_base+0x104 | unwind-pe.h:265 -> unwind-pe.h:255
134	  876 | 0x000000000004bc5e -> 0x000000000004bd00 | classify_object_over_fdes+0x46 -> classify_object_over_fdes+0xe8        | unwind-dw2-fde.c:738 -> unwind-dw2-fde.c:732 (discriminator 1)
135	  876 | 0x000000000004aae4 -> 0x000000000004bcdc | read_encoded_value_with_base+0x0 -> classify_object_over_fdes+0xc4      | unwind-pe.h:203 -> unwind-dw2-fde.c:781
136	  869 | 0x000000000004bcf2 -> 0x000000000004bd64 | classify_object_over_fdes+0xda -> classify_object_over_fdes+0x14c       | unwind-dw2-fde.c:786 -> unwind-dw2-fde.c:793

140

142	  500 are the per-call exits (last iter of `dot` falls through instead of
143	  branching back).
144	- `start_pc = 0x1056e`, `end_pc = 0x1057c` - the header and backward
145	  branch of the inner loop.
146	- `location` - both PCs are inside `dot`, header at `+0xc`, tail at `+0x1a`.
147	- `source` - both map to `hotloops_demo.c:6`, which is exactly
148	  `for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) s += a[i] * b[i];`.

156	- Strip rarely-interesting libc/startup loops by raising `--min-iters`
157	  to a value above your background noise floor (e.g. `--min-iters 1000`
158	  for a workload that runs your hot path millions of times).
159	- For a very short workload, lower `--min-iters` and raise `--limit`
160	  to see the full distribution.

file.content:
# hotloops - hot inner-loop detector

Detects the hottest loops in a RISC-V ELF by observing backward
translation-block transitions in QEMU. For each detected loop you get
the loop header PC, the backward-branch PC, and the iteration count.
With DWARF, you also get the source `file:line`.

Two parts ship together:

- `contrib/plugins/hotloops.c` - QEMU TCG plugin that records edges.
- `scripts/hotloops.sh` - wrapper that runs the plugin and formats output.

## Usage

```
scripts/hotloops.sh [options] <elf> [--] [program args...]
```

### Options

| Flag                | Default | Description                                                    |
|---------------------|---------|----------------------------------------------------------------|
| `--arch <a>`        | auto    | Target arch: `riscv32`, `riscv64`, or `auto` (from `file`).    |
| `--qemu <path>`     | auto    | Override the QEMU binary.                                      |
| `--plugin <path>`   | auto    | Override the plugin `.so`.                                     |
| `--limit <N>`       | 50      | Print at most N loops.                                         |
| `--min-iters <N>`   | 1       | Drop loops with fewer than N iterations (hot-loop threshold).  |
| `--objdump <path>`  | auto    | Override objdump (for `function+offset` resolution).           |
| `--addr2line <path>`| auto    | Override addr2line (for source line resolution).               |
| `--no-source`       | off     | Skip source resolution even if DWARF is present.               |
| `--raw`             | off     | Print raw plugin output (CSV-ish), no formatting.              |

### Build prerequisites

The plugin lives in `contrib/plugins/`. Build once:

```
ninja -C build contrib-plugins
```

It is incremental on subsequent edits to `hotloops.c`. The QEMU binaries
themselves (`qemu-riscv64`, `qemu-riscv32`) do **not** need to be rebuilt.

## How it detects a loop

For every translated block, the plugin remembers the PC of its last
instruction. When the next-executed block starts at a PC `<=` that
remembered PC, the transition is a backward edge - i.e. a loop iteration.
The pair `(header_pc, tail_pc)` is the key; the count is the number of
iterations.

This catches every loop a real backward branch closes (for/while/do-while,
goto, manually unrolled tails, etc.) without needing to model branch
semantics.

Caveat: each iteration counted is one *taken* backward branch. A loop
that runs N times has N-1 taken backward branches plus one fall-through
on exit, so the reported count is N-1 per call to the enclosing function.

## Output format

Header line, then a fixed-width table sorted by `iters` descending:

```
# hot loops (iters >= <threshold>), sorted desc, top <K>
iters | start_pc           -> end_pc             | location              | source
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
<n>   | 0x<16-hex>         -> 0x<16-hex>         | <fn>+0x<o> -> <fn>+0x<o> | file:line -> file:line
...
```

Columns:

- `iters`     - taken backward-branch count (sorted desc).
- `start_pc`  - PC of the loop header (the branch target).
- `end_pc`    - PC of the backward branch (the loop tail).
- `location`  - `function+offset -> function+offset` from the ELF symbol table.
                Shows `?` if the address is not inside any sized FUNC symbol.
- `source`    - `file:line -> file:line` from DWARF. Collapsed to a single
                `file:line` when both ends map to the same source line.
                Only present when the ELF has debug info and `--no-source`
                is not set.

The `--raw` mode prints the unformatted plugin output instead:

```
hotloops: <distinct> distinct backward edges, <hot> hot (>= <thr> iters), sorted by iterations desc
start_pc, end_pc, iterations
0x..., 0x..., <n>
...
```

## Example

Source:

```c
/* /tmp/hotloops_demo.c */
#include <stdio.h>

__attribute__((noinline))
static int dot(volatile const int *a, volatile const int *b, int n) {
    int s = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) s += a[i] * b[i];   /* line 6 */
    return s;
}

int main(void) {
    int a[64], b[64];
    for (int i = 0; i < 64; i++) { a[i] = i; b[i] = 64 - i; }
    long total = 0;
    for (int outer = 0; outer < 500; outer++)
        total += dot(a, b, 64);                      /* 64 * 500 inner iters */
    printf("total=%ld\n", total);
    return 0;
}
```

Build with debug info and run:

```
$ riscv64-linux-gnu-gcc -O1 -g -static /tmp/hotloops_demo.c -o /tmp/hotloops_demo
$ scripts/hotloops.sh --limit 5 --min-iters 100 /tmp/hotloops_demo
```

Output:

```
# hot loops (iters >= 100), sorted desc, top 5
iters | start_pc           -> end_pc             | location                                                                | source
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
31500 | 0x000000000001056e -> 0x000000000001057c | dot+0xc -> dot+0x1a                                                     | hotloops_demo.c:6 (discriminator 3) -> hotloops_demo.c:6 (discriminator 1)
 1757 | 0x000000000004ab54 -> 0x000000000004abe8 | read_encoded_value_with_base+0x70 -> read_encoded_value_with_base+0x104 | unwind-pe.h:265 -> unwind-pe.h:255
  876 | 0x000000000004bc5e -> 0x000000000004bd00 | classify_object_over_fdes+0x46 -> classify_object_over_fdes+0xe8        | unwind-dw2-fde.c:738 -> unwind-dw2-fde.c:732 (discriminator 1)
  876 | 0x000000000004aae4 -> 0x000000000004bcdc | read_encoded_value_with_base+0x0 -> classify_object_over_fdes+0xc4      | unwind-pe.h:203 -> unwind-dw2-fde.c:781
  869 | 0x000000000004bcf2 -> 0x000000000004bd64 | classify_object_over_fdes+0xda -> classify_object_over_fdes+0x14c       | unwind-dw2-fde.c:786 -> unwind-dw2-fde.c:793
```

Reading the top row:

- `iters = 31500` - close to the expected `64 * 500 = 32000`; the missing
  500 are the per-call exits (last iter of `dot` falls through instead of
  branching back).
- `start_pc = 0x1056e`, `end_pc = 0x1057c` - the header and backward
  branch of the inner loop.
- `location` - both PCs are inside `dot`, header at `+0xc`, tail at `+0x1a`.
- `source` - both map to `hotloops_demo.c:6`, which is exactly
  `for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) s += a[i] * b[i];`.

The remaining rows are glibc unwinder loops that ran during static init.
The 500-iter outer loop (`main+0x38 -> main+0x3a`) was filtered out by
`--min-iters 100` - well below the inner loop's contribution.

## Tips

- Strip rarely-interesting libc/startup loops by raising `--min-iters`
  to a value above your background noise floor (e.g. `--min-iters 1000`
  for a workload that runs your hot path millions of times).
- For a very short workload, lower `--min-iters` and raise `--limit`
  to see the full distribution.
- Pass program arguments after `--`, e.g.
  `scripts/hotloops.sh ./prog -- --bench fast`.
- `--raw` is useful when piping into another tool; the format is stable.

- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. Target VPU, toolchain, build flags, FPGA board, and hardware profile all come from config.toml — no hardcoded paths or CPU names. Runs a 7-step FSM — validate, select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
- hot-inner-kernel-extractor: Extract a libnn function's hot inner-most loop into a standalone, mcycle-wrapped RISC-V assembly harness so it can be measured on a cycle-accurate Verilator simulator (or any cycle counter) without running the whole function. Use this skill whenever the user wants to study pipeline behavior of a libnn kernel, profile inner-loop cycles, build a microbenchmark from an existing function, prepare a Verilator workload, isolate a hot loop body, or asks about "extracting", "isolating", "harnessing", "microbenchmark", "Verilator timeview", "pipeline timeview", "steady state", or "inner kernel" of any libnn / RVV function. The skill identifies the hottest translation block via QEMU's hotblocks plugin, walks objdump to find the innermost backward branch, computes live-in registers, and emits a compilable .S harness that runs the body N times under csrr mcycle.
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
Hot loops in `t_conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any.adx` (iters >= 1000):

| iters | location | role |
|---|---|---|
| 32268 | `test+0x358 -> test+0x37c` | test-driver loop (verify outer; not perf-relevant) |
| 5280 | `memcpy+0xac -> memcpy+0xfe` | im2col / buffer staging |
| **3976** | `nn_mat_mult_kernel_s8_offset_unroll4+0x700 -> +0x7fa` | **GEMM tail / cleanup loop** |
| **3808** | `riscv_nn_conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any+0x82c -> +0x850` | **conv outer (height/width tile)** |
| **2008** | `nn_mat_mult_kernel_s8_offset_unroll4+0xe0 -> +0x846` | **GEMM main inner kernel (4-row unroll)** |
| 1920 | `riscv_nn_conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any+0x6c4 -> +0x84a` | conv im2col / pad-handling outer |
| 1610 | `riscv_nn_conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any+0x6b4 -> +0x6d8` | inner pad-fill loop |
| 1488 | `nn_mat_mult_kernel_s8_offset_unroll4+0x80 -> +0x2c8` | GEMM body fragment |

## Reading

The conv dispatches to **`nn_mat_mult_kernel_s8_offset_unroll4`** — that's the actual hot work. Two distinct loop structures inside it:
- **+0xe0 -> +0x846** (2008 iters, large span) — the unrolled-by-4 main GEMM body.
- **+0x700 -> +0x7fa** (3976 iters, smaller span) — the per-row cleanup / reduction tail that runs more often (one per row, vs the main loop running once per row of 4).

Caveat: no `source` column because the QEMU verify build doesn't carry DWARF info (default `-O3` without `-g`). To get `file:line`, rebuild adding `-g` to `${CFG_CFLAGS_VERIFY}`. Also note these are **QEMU iteration counts for a verify-flag build with 3 sub-tests** — not the FPGA-build hot-loop distribution. For optimization decisions, weight against the FPGA cycle profile.

# /optimize skill configuration - template
#
# Copy this to config.toml and edit for your target. The skill NEVER reads
# literal paths, flags, VLENs, hostnames, or CPU names from SKILL.md or from
# scripts under script/. Everything flows through this file.
#
# Adding a new VPU configuration:
#   1. Drop the Andes Verilog config file under fpga_config/
#   2. If the microarchitecture family is new, add references/uarch/<soc>.md
#   3. Edit [target] to point at the new .inc and uarch doc
#   4. Adjust [target.cflags_base] and [qemu].cpu / [qemu].vlen
#
# No SKILL.md or script edits should ever be needed for a new target.

################################################################################
# [repo] - where libnn source lives and where FSM state is persisted
################################################################################
[repo]
# Absolute path to the libnn source tree (build.sh, Source/, Include/, etc.)
libnn_root = "/home/nick/work/libnn_0421"

# State file location, relative to libnn_root (skill writes FSM state here)
state_file = ".claude/skills/optimize/state.json"

# Work directory for intermediate artifacts (compiled ELFs, objdumps, logs),
# relative to libnn_root
work_dir = ".claude/skills/optimize/work"


################################################################################
# [toolchain] - cross-compiler
################################################################################
[toolchain]
bin_dir = "/local/nick/SW_Release/build-ast540/build-toolchain/linux/nds64le-elf-newlib-v5d/bin"
prefix  = "riscv64-elf-"


################################################################################
# [target] - which VPU, its hardware profile, and its build flags
################################################################################
[target]
# Target CPU name (passed to build.sh)
cpu = "AX45MPV"

# Andes Verilog config file (parsed for VLEN, caches, ISA feature flags).
# Relative to the skill directory (.claude/skills/optimize/).
fpga_config = "fpga_config/ax45mpv_v1024_8mb.inc"

# Microarchitecture reference doc (pipeline model, stall taxonomy).
# Proposer subagent reads this during `analyze`. Relative to skill dir.
uarch_ref = "references/uarch/andes_45_series.md"

# Intrinsic API reference doc (which macros are legal in new RVV code).
# Implement subagent reads this. Relative to skill dir.
intrinsic_api = "references/intrinsic_api/nds_vec_macros.md"


# CFLAGS assembled from three sources (order preserved, concatenated):
#   cflags_base    - baseline compile flags (both verify and perf builds)
#   cflags_perf    - extra flags for FPGA perf build (adds PF_COUNTER, etc.)
#   cflags_verify  - extra flags for QEMU correctness build

[target.cflags_base]
# Flags extracted from build.sh's AX45MPV block
flags = [
  "-O3", "-ffunction-sections", "-fdata-sections", "-Wall", "-Werror",
  "-mtune=andes-45-series", "-fno-strict-aliasing",
  "-mext-vector", "-DENA_VEC_ISA",
  "-DMAX_VLEN=1024", "-DNDS_VEC_RVV_VERSION=1000",
  "-DENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD",
  "-fno-tree-slp-vectorize", "-fno-tree-vectorize",
]

[target.cflags_perf]
flags = [
  "-DPF_COUNTER", "-DENA_TEST_PERF", "-DENA_MEASURE_PERF=1",
  "-DENA_RUN_TWICE", "-DENA_CACHEABLE_ENV_SETUP",
  "-mvh",
]

[target.cflags_verify]
flags = [
  "-DENA_FAST_ALGO", "-DENA_DUMP_GOLDEN=0", "-DENA_MEASURE_PERF=0",
  "-mext-vector=zve64d", "-mvh",
]


[target.active_defines]
# Macros analyze/implement subagents treat as defined or undefined for this
# build (governs which #ifdef branches are live source vs dead code).
defined   = ["ENA_VEC_ISA", "ENA_TILING", "ENA_FAST_ALGO",
             "ENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD"]
undefined = ["ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC", "ENA_VEC_ISA_ZVQMAC"]


################################################################################
# [qemu] - correctness simulator (ground truth: accuracy only, not perf)
################################################################################
[qemu]
binary  = "/local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv64"
machine = "andes_ae350"
cpu     = "andes-ax45mpv,vext_spec=v1.0,vlen=1024"
# QEMU device config, relative to libnn_root
config  = "project/ax45mpv/qemu_cfg/zve64d/ADP-AE350-AX45MPV-1C_dw0_mw1.cfg"
vlen    = 1024
timeout = 300


################################################################################
# [fpga] - ground truth for cycle counts
################################################################################
[fpga]
host    = "sw-boards.andestech.com"
port    = 1113
timeout = 300

# Linker script + sim-wrapper + reset flag, all relative to libnn_root
linker_script   = "project/ax45mpv/ae350-xip_hvm.ld"
sim_wrapper     = "project/ax45mpv/riscv64-sim-wrapper-on-board"
reset_flag_file = "project/ax45mpv/First_Reset_done"


################################################################################
# [vsim] - cycle-accurate Verilator simulator (used by hot-inner-kernel-extractor)
#
# Bare-metal simulator targeting AX45MPV. Differs from FPGA: standalone ELF
# (no GDB load), no nds_pfcounter.h (we use raw csrr mcycle/minstret), and
# stdout goes through HTIF via libgloss. Cycle counts are RTL-cycle-accurate
# but very slow (~9 kHz) -- which is why we extract just the hot inner kernel
# instead of running the whole libnn perf test.
################################################################################
[vsim]
binary       = "/local/nick/vsim/build/sim_ax45mpv_premium"
# CFLAGS for vsim builds. rv64gcv_zvfh_zfh_xandes covers RVV 1.0 + Andes
# vendor extensions (nds.vd4dots, nds.lea.h, etc.). -O1 matches the
# vsim-demo/acervv example -- the kernel.s is hand-emitted so the C
# wrapper's optimization level barely matters.
# Why rv64gc_zve32x_xandes (not the more permissive rv64gcv): with full `v`
# enabled, GCC emits e64 m8 vle64.v inside libc helpers (e.g. _fstat ->
# SYSCALL_2 path) and our vsim build (sim_ax45mpv_premium) traps
# illegal-instruction on that combo (mcause=2). The vsim-demo/rvv example
# uses zve32x and runs cleanly, so we mirror it. _xandes is mandatory --
# kernel.s contains nds.vd4dots which lives in the Andes vendor extension.
# -fno-tree-{slp-,}vectorize is belt-and-braces: keeps GCC from auto-
# vectorizing the C wrapper.
cflags       = "-march=rv64gc_zve32x_xandes -O1 -fno-tree-vectorize -fno-tree-slp-vectorize"
# Linker flags from vsim-demo/Make.var. Static link, large stack at 0x3000000
# so deep call chains in libgloss/printf don't blow the default 4 KB stack.
ldflags      = "-static -Wl,--defsym,_stack=0x3000000"
# Bare-metal startup: skip toolchain's default crt0 in favor of the
# vsim-demo crt0 (sets up HTIF and trap vector before main).
startfiles   = "-nostartfiles"
# Runtime sources, relative to the hot-inner-kernel-extractor skill dir.
# These come from /local/nick/vsim-workspace/vsim-demo/ and are vendored
# into vsim_runtime/ so the skill builds without needing that workspace
# checked out on every machine.
runtime_dir  = "vsim_runtime"
runtime_files = ["crt0.S", "libgloss.c", "handler.c", "trap.S"]
timeout      = 1800


################################################################################
# [limits] - FSM exit conditions and cache TTLs
################################################################################
[limits]
# Max optimization rounds per function
max_rounds = 10
# Re-validation cache: skip validate state if config + .inc unchanged and
# last validation was more recent than this (hours). 0 = always re-validate.
validate_cache_hours = 24


################################################################################
# [meta_harness] - Phase 0 trace emission for the outer harness-critic loop
################################################################################
[meta_harness]
# Master toggle for emit_trace.sh. When false, all `init`/`step`/`timing`/`fail`
# calls short-circuit with exit 0 and write nothing to trace_corpus/. FSM
# behavior is unaffected (trace emission is additive by design).
trace_enabled = false

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# /optimize skill configuration - template
#
# Copy this to config.toml and edit for your target. The skill NEVER reads
# literal paths, flags, VLENs, hostnames, or CPU names from SKILL.md or from
# scripts under script/. Everything flows through this file.
#
# Adding a new VPU configuration:
#   1. Drop the Andes Verilog config file under fpga_config/
#   2. If the microarchitecture family is new, add references/uarch/<soc>.md
#   3. Edit [target] to point at the new .inc and uarch doc
#   4. Adjust [target.cflags_base] and [qemu].cpu / [qemu].vlen
#
# No SKILL.md or script edits should ever be needed for a new target.

################################################################################
# [repo] - where libnn source lives and where FSM state is persisted
################################################################################
[repo]
# Absolute path to the libnn source tree (build.sh, Source/, Include/, etc.)
libnn_root = "/home/nick/work/libnn_0421"

# State file location, relative to libnn_root (skill writes FSM state here)
state_file = ".claude/skills/optimize/state.json"

# Work directory for intermediate artifacts (compiled ELFs, objdumps, logs),
# relative to libnn_root
work_dir = ".claude/skills/optimize/work"


################################################################################
# [toolchain] - cross-compiler
################################################################################
[toolchain]
bin_dir = "/local/nick/SW_Release/build-ast540/build-toolchain/linux/nds64le-elf-newlib-v5d/bin"
prefix  = "riscv64-elf-"


################################################################################
# [target] - which VPU, its hardware profile, and its build flags
################################################################################
[target]
# Target CPU name (passed to build.sh)
cpu = "AX45MPV"

# Andes Verilog config file (parsed for VLEN, caches, ISA feature flags).
# Relative to the skill directory (.claude/skills/optimize/).
fpga_config = "fpga_config/ax45mpv_v1024_8mb.inc"

# Microarchitecture reference doc (pipeline model, stall taxonomy).
# Proposer subagent reads this during `analyze`. Relative to skill dir.
uarch_ref = "references/uarch/andes_45_series.md"

# Intrinsic API reference doc (which macros are legal in new RVV code).
# Implement subagent reads this. Relative to skill dir.
intrinsic_api = "references/intrinsic_api/nds_vec_macros.md"


# CFLAGS assembled from three sources (order preserved, concatenated):
#   cflags_base    - baseline compile flags (both verify and perf builds)
#   cflags_perf    - extra flags for FPGA perf build (adds PF_COUNTER, etc.)
#   cflags_verify  - extra flags for QEMU correctness build

[target.cflags_base]
# Flags extracted from build.sh's AX45MPV block
flags = [
  "-O3", "-ffunction-sections", "-fdata-sections", "-Wall", "-Werror",
  "-mtune=andes-45-series", "-fno-strict-aliasing",
  "-mext-vector", "-DENA_VEC_ISA",
  "-DMAX_VLEN=1024", "-DNDS_VEC_RVV_VERSION=1000",
  "-DENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD",
  "-fno-tree-slp-vectorize", "-fno-tree-vectorize",
]

[target.cflags_perf]
flags = [
  "-DPF_COUNTER", "-DENA_TEST_PERF", "-DENA_MEASURE_PERF=1",
  "-DENA_RUN_TWICE", "-DENA_CACHEABLE_ENV_SETUP",
  "-mvh",
]

[target.cflags_verify]
flags = [
  "-DENA_FAST_ALGO", "-DENA_DUMP_GOLDEN=0", "-DENA_MEASURE_PERF=0",
  "-mext-vector=zve64d", "-mvh",
  "-g",
]


[target.active_defines]
# Macros analyze/implement subagents treat as defined or undefined for this
# build (governs which #ifdef branches are live source vs dead code).
defined   = ["ENA_VEC_ISA", "ENA_TILING", "ENA_FAST_ALGO",
             "ENA_NDS_V5_VEC_DOT_PROD"]
undefined = ["ENA_VEC_INTRINSIC", "ENA_VEC_ISA_ZVQMAC"]


################################################################################
# [qemu] - correctness simulator (ground truth: accuracy only, not perf)
################################################################################
[qemu]
binary  = "/local/nick/qemu_v5/build/qemu-system-riscv64"
machine = "andes_ae350"
cpu     = "andes-ax45mpv,vext_spec=v1.0,vlen=1024"
# QEMU device config, relative to libnn_root
config  = "project/ax45mpv/qemu_cfg/zve64d/ADP-AE350-AX45MPV-1C_dw0_mw1.cfg"
vlen    = 1024
timeout = 300


################################################################################
# [fpga] - ground truth for cycle counts
################################################################################
[fpga]
host    = "sw-boards.andestech.com"
port    = 1113
timeout = 300

# Linker script + sim-wrapper + reset flag, all relative to libnn_root
linker_script   = "project/ax45mpv/ae350-xip_hvm.ld"
sim_wrapper     = "project/ax45mpv/riscv64-sim-wrapper-on-board"
reset_flag_file = "project/ax45mpv/First_Reset_done"


################################################################################

startfiles   = "-nostartfiles"
# Runtime sources, relative to the hot-inner-kernel-extractor skill dir.
# These come from /local/nick/vsim-workspace/vsim-demo/ and are vendored

runtime_dir  = "vsim_runtime"
runtime_files = ["crt0.S", "libgloss.c", "handler.c", "trap.S"]
timeout      = 1800


################################################################################
# [limits] - FSM exit conditions and cache TTLs
################################################################################
[limits]
# Max optimization rounds per function
max_rounds = 10
# Re-validation cache: skip validate state if config + .inc unchanged and
# last validation was more recent than this (hours). 0 = always re-validate.
validate_cache_hours = 24


################################################################################
# [meta_harness] - Phase 0 trace emission for the outer harness-critic loop
################################################################################
[meta_harness]
# Master toggle for emit_trace.sh. When false, all `init`/`step`/`timing`/`fail`

content.text: For DWARF on libnn functions (not just `test()`), `libnn.a` must also be built with `-g`. Adding to base flags.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. Target VPU, toolchain, build flags, FPGA board, and hardware profile all come from config.toml — no hardcoded paths or CPU names. Runs a 7-step FSM — validate, select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
- hot-inner-kernel-extractor: Extract a libnn function's hot inner-most loop into a standalone, mcycle-wrapped RISC-V assembly harness so it can be measured on a cycle-accurate Verilator simulator (or any cycle counter) without running the whole function. Use this skill whenever the user wants to study pipeline behavior of a libnn kernel, profile inner-loop cycles, build a microbenchmark from an existing function, prepare a Verilator workload, isolate a hot loop body, or asks about "extracting", "isolating", "harnessing", "microbenchmark", "Verilator timeview", "pipeline timeview", "steady state", or "inner kernel" of any libnn / RVV function. The skill identifies the hottest translation block via QEMU's hotblocks plugin, walks objdump to find the innermost backward branch, computes live-in registers, and emits a compilable .S harness that runs the body N times under csrr mcycle.
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- optimize: Fully autonomous RVV optimization loop for libnn functions. Target VPU, toolchain, build flags, FPGA board, and hardware profile all come from config.toml — no hardcoded paths or CPU names. Runs a 7-step FSM — validate, select, analyze, implement, verify, measure, learn — then loops back to analyze with new insights. No human intervention required.
- hot-inner-kernel-extractor: Extract a libnn function's hot inner-most loop into a standalone, mcycle-wrapped RISC-V assembly harness so it can be measured on a cycle-accurate Verilator simulator (or any cycle counter) without running the whole function. Use this skill whenever the user wants to study pipeline behavior of a libnn kernel, profile inner-loop cycles, build a microbenchmark from an existing function, prepare a Verilator workload, isolate a hot loop body, or asks about "extracting", "isolating", "harnessing", "microbenchmark", "Verilator timeview", "pipeline timeview", "steady state", or "inner kernel" of any libnn / RVV function. The skill identifies the hottest translation block via QEMU's hotblocks plugin, walks objdump to find the innermost backward branch, computes live-in registers, and emits a compilable .S harness that runs the body N times under csrr mcycle.

| iters | location | source |
|---|---|---|
| 32268 | `test+0x358 -> test+0x37c` | `test_util.h:191 -> :189` (verify accuracy-check loop, not perf-relevant) |
| 5280 | `memcpy+0xac -> memcpy+0xfe` | libc, `?` (no DWARF) |
| **3976** | `nn_mat_mult_kernel_s8_offset_unroll4+0x700 -> +0x7fa` | **`nn_mat_mult_kernel_s8_offset.c:1865 -> :1862`** -- GEMM tail |
| **3808** | `riscv_nn_conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any+0x82c -> +0x850` | **`riscv_nn_conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any.c:584 -> riscv_nn_support.h:138`** -- conv outer dispatch |
| **2008** | `nn_mat_mult_kernel_s8_offset_unroll4+0xe0 -> +0x846` | **`nn_mat_mult_kernel_s8_offset.c:1963 -> :1918`** -- GEMM main 4-row body |
| 1920 | `riscv_nn_conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any+0x6c4 -> +0x84a` | `riscv_nn_conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any.c:582` -- conv im2col loop |
| 1610 | `riscv_nn_conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any+0x6b4 -> +0x6d8` | `:557 -> :579` -- conv pad-fill |
| 1488 | `nn_mat_mult_kernel_s8_offset_unroll4+0x80 -> +0x2c8` | `nn_mat_mult_kernel_s8_offset.c:1777 -> :1767` -- GEMM body fragment |

## Hot path

The optimization target is **`nn_mat_mult_kernel_s8_offset_unroll4` lines 1763-1965** in `Source/NNSupportFunctions/nn_mat_mult_kernel_s8_offset.c`. Two hot regions:
- **lines 1918-1963** -- the unrolled-by-4 main GEMM body (2008 iters)
- **lines 1862-1865** -- per-row reduction tail (3976 iters; runs more frequently because tail is per-row while main loop is per-4-rows)

The conv function itself (`riscv_nn_conv_HWC_s8_s8_s8_asym_bias_any.c:557, 579, 582, 584`) is just the im2col + dispatch wrapper around the GEMM kernel.
